This is partly a micro-optimization, but it's\\n735:         // also an opportunity to \\\"sub-unify\\\" the variables. This isn't\\n736:         // *necessary* to prevent cycles, because they would eventually be sub-unified\\n737:         // anyhow during generalization, but it helps with diagnostics (we can detect\\n738:         // earlier that they are sub-unified).\\n739:         //\\n740:         // Note that we can just skip the binders here because\\n741:         // type variables can't (at present, Err((a_vid, b_vid));\\n752:             }\\n753:             _ => {}\\n754:         }\\n755: \\n756:         self.enter_forall(predicate, |ty::SubtypePredicate { a_is_expected, a, b }| {\\n757: This is partly a micro-optimization, but it's\\n735:         // also an opportunity to \\\"sub-unify\\\" the variables. This isn't\\n736:         // *necessary* to prevent cycles, because they would eventually be sub-unified\\n737:         // anyhow during generalization, but it helps with diagnostics (we can detect\\n738:         // earlier that they are sub-unified).\\n739:         //\\n740:         // Note that we can just skip the binders here because\\n741:         // type variables can't (at present, at\\n742:         // least) capture any of the things bound by this binder.\\n743:         //\\n744:         // Note that this sub here is not just for diagnostics - it has semantic\\n745:         // effects as well.\\n746:         let r_a = self.shallow_resolve(predicate.skip_binder().a);\\n747:         let r_b = self.shallow_resolve(predicate.skip_binder().b);\\n748:         match (r_a.kind(), r_b.kind()) {\\n749:             (&ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(a_vid)), &ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(b_vid))) => {\\n750:                 self.sub_unify_ty_vids_raw(a_vid, b_vid);\\n751:                 return Err((a_vid, b_vid));\\n752:             }\\n753:             _ => {}\\n754:         }\\n755: \\n756:         self.enter_forall(predicate, |ty::SubtypePredicate { a_is_expected, a, b }| {\\n757: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/rust--01f6ddf7@525258043d7f9dcc1b69d9abcb9bfea32826a219/-/blob/compiler/rustc_infer/src/infer/mod.rs%3FL715-765\"}","structuredContent":{"content":"715:         cause: &ObligationCause<'tcx>,\n716:         param_env: ty::ParamEnv<'tcx>,\n717:         predicate: ty::PolyCoercePredicate<'tcx>,\n718:     ) -> Result<InferResult<'tcx, ()>, (TyVid, TyVid)> {\n719:         let subtype_predicate = predicate.map_bound(|p| ty::SubtypePredicate {\n720:             a_is_expected: false, // when coercing from `a` to `b`, `b` is expected\n721:             a: p.a,\n722:             b: p.b,\n723:         });\n724:         self.subtype_predicate(cause, param_env, subtype_predicate)\n725:     }\n726: \n727:     pub fn subtype_predicate(\n728:         &self,\n729:         cause: &ObligationCause<'tcx>,\n730:         param_env: ty::ParamEnv<'tcx>,\n731:         predicate: ty::PolySubtypePredicate<'tcx>,\n732:     ) -> Result<InferResult<'tcx, ()>, (TyVid, TyVid)> {\n733:         // Check for two unresolved inference variables, in which case we can\n734:         // make no progress. This is partly a micro-optimization, but it's\n735:         // also an opportunity to \"sub-unify\" the variables. This isn't\n736:         // *necessary* to prevent cycles, because they would eventually be sub-unified\n737:         // anyhow during generalization, but it helps with diagnostics (we can detect\n738:         // earlier that they are sub-unified).\n739:         //\n740:         // Note that we can just skip the binders here because\n741:         // type variables can't (at present, at\n742:         // least) capture any of the things bound by this binder.\n743:         //\n744:         // Note that this sub here is not just for diagnostics - it has semantic\n745:         // effects as well.\n746:         let r_a = self.shallow_resolve(predicate.skip_binder().a);\n747:         let r_b = self.shallow_resolve(predicate.skip_binder().b);\n748:         match (r_a.kind(), r_b.kind()) {\n749:             (&ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(a_vid)), &ty::Infer(ty::TyVar(b_vid))) => {\n750:                 self.sub_unify_ty_vids_raw(a_vid, b_vid);\n751:                 return Err((a_vid, b_vid));\n752:             }\n753:             _ => {}\n754:         }\n755: \n756:         self.enter_forall(predicate, |ty::SubtypePredicate { a_is_expected, a, b }| {\n757:             if a_is_expected {\n758:                 Ok(self.at(cause, param_env).sub(DefineOpaqueTypes::Yes, a, b))\n759:             } else {\n760:                 Ok(self.at(cause, param_env).sup(DefineOpaqueTypes::Yes, b, a))\n761:             }\n762:         })\n763:     }\n764: \n765:     /// Number of type variables created so far.\n766: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/rust--01f6ddf7@525258043d7f9dcc1b69d9abcb9bfea32826a219/-/blob/compiler/rustc_infer/src/infer/mod.rs%3FL715-765"}}}

These take numerous guises in syntax,\\n44: /// but perhaps the most recognizable form is in a where-clause:\\n45: /// ```ignore (illustrative)\\n46: /// T: Foo<U>\\n47: /// ```\\n48: /// This would Self::new_from_args(interner, trait_def_id, args)\\n83:     }\\n84: \\n85:     pub fn from_assoc(interner: I, trait_id: I::TraitId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> TraitRef<I> {\\n86:         let generics = }\\n187: }\\n188: \\n189: impl<I: Interner> UpcastFrom<I, ty::Binder<I, TraitRef<I>>> for ty::Binder<I, TraitPredicate<I>> {\\n190:     fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<I, TraitRef<I>>, _tcx: I) -> for Type`\\n213:     Negative,\\n214:     /// `#[rustc_reservation_impl] impl Trait for Type`\\n215:     ///\\n216:     /// This is a \\\"stability hack\\\", not a real Rust feature.\\n217:     /// See #64631 for details.\\n218:     Reservation,\\n219: }\\n220: \\n221: impl fmt::Display for ImplPolarity {\\n222:     fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\\n223:         match self {\\n224:             Self::Positive => f.write_str(\\\"positive\\\"),\\n225:             Self::Negative => f.write_str(\\\"negative\\\"),\\n226:             Self::Reservation => f.write_str(\\\"reservation\\\"),\\n227:         }\\n228:     }\\n229: }\\n230: \\n231: impl ImplPolarity {\\n232:     /// The polarity marker in front of the impl trait ref if applicable.\\n233:     pub fn as_str(self) -> &'static str {\\n234:         match self {\\n235:             Self::Positive => \\\"\\\",\\n236:             Self::Negative => \\\"!\\\",\\n237:             Self::Reservation => \\\"\\\",\\n238:         }\\n239:     }\\n240: }\\n241: \\n242: /// Polarity for a trait predicate. May either be negative or positive.\\n243: /// Distinguished from [`ImplPolarity`] since we never compute goals with\\n244: /// \\\"reservation\\\" level.\\n245: #[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Debug)]\\n246: #[cfg_attr(\\n247:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n248:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n249: )]\\n250: pub enum PredicatePolarity {\\n251:     /// `Type: Trait`\\n252:     Positive,\\n253:     /// `Type: !Trait`\\n254:     Negative,\\n255: }\\n256: \\n257: impl PredicatePolarity {\\n258:     /// Flips polarity by turning `Positive` into `Negative` and `Negative` into `Positive`.\\n259:     pub fn flip(&self) -> PredicatePolarity {\\n260:         match self {\\n261:             PredicatePolarity::Positive => PredicatePolarity::Negative,\\n262:             PredicatePolarity::Negative => PredicatePolarity::Positive,\\n263:         }\\n264:     }\\n265: }\\n266: \\n267: impl fmt::Display for PredicatePolarity {\\n268:     fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\\n269:         match self {\\n270:             Self::Positive => f.write_str(\\\"positive\\\"),\\n271:             Self::Negative => f.write_str(\\\"negative\\\"),\\n272:         }\\n273:     }\\n274: }\\n275: \\n276: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\\n277: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n278: #[cfg_attr(\\n279:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n280:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n281: )]\\n282: pub enum ExistentialPredicate<I: Interner> {\\n283:     /// E.g., `Iterator`.\\n284:     Trait(ExistentialTraitRef<I>),\\n285:     /// E.g., `Iterator::Item = T`.\\n286:     Projection(ExistentialProjection<I>),\\n287:     /// E.g., `Send`.\\n288:     AutoTrait(I::TraitId),\\n289: }\\n290: \\n291: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialPredicate<I> {}\\n292: \\n293: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialPredicate<I>> {\\n294:     /// Given an existential predicate like `?Self: PartialEq<u32>` (e.g., derived from `dyn PartialEq<u32>`),\\n295:     /// and a concrete type `self_ty`, returns a full predicate where the existentially quantified variable `?Self`\\n296:     /// has been replaced with `self_ty` (e.g., `self_ty: PartialEq<u32>`, in our example).\\n297:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> I::Clause {\\n298:         match self.skip_binder() {\\n299:             ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => self.rebind(tr).with_self_ty(cx, self_ty).upcast(cx),\\n300:             ExistentialPredicate::Projection(p) => {\\n301:                 self.rebind(p.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty)).upcast(cx)\\n302:             }\\n303:             ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => {\\n304:                 let generics = cx.generics_of(did.into());\\n305:                 let trait_ref = if generics.count() == 1 {\\n306:                     ty::TraitRef::new(cx, did, [self_ty])\\n307:                 } else {\\n308:                     // If this is an ill-formed auto trait, then synthesize\\n309:                     // new error args for the missing generics.\\n310:                     let err_args =\\n311:                         GenericArgs::extend_with_error(cx, did.into(), &[self_ty.into()]);\\n312:                     ty::TraitRef::new_from_args(cx, did, err_args)\\n313:                 };\\n314:                 self.rebind(trait_ref).upcast(cx)\\n315:             }\\n316:         }\\n317:     }\\n318: }\\n319: \\n320: /// An existential reference to a trait, where `Self` is erased.\\n321: /// For example, the trait object `Trait<'a, 'b, X, Y>` is:\\n322: /// ```ignore (illustrative)\\n323: /// exists T. T: Trait<'a, 'b, X, Y>\\n324: /// ```\\n325: /// The generic parameters don't include the erased `Self`, only trait\\n326: /// type and lifetime parameters (`[X, Y]` and `['a, 'b]` above).\\n327: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\\n328: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n329: #[cfg_attr(\\n330:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n331:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n332: )]\\n333: pub struct ExistentialTraitRef<I: Interner> {\\n334:     pub def_id: I::TraitId,\\n335:     pub args: I::GenericArgs,\\n336:     /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `ExistentialTraitRef` without\\n337:     /// calling [`ExistentialTraitRef::new_from_args`].\\n338:     _use_existential_trait_ref_new_instead: (),\\n339: }\\n340: \\n341: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialTraitRef<I> {}\\n342: \\n343: impl<I: Interner> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\\n344:     pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, trait_def_id: I::TraitId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> Self {\\n345:         interner.debug_assert_existential_args_compatible(trait_def_id.into(), args);\\n346:         Self { def_id: trait_def_id, args, _use_existential_trait_ref_new_instead: () }\\n347:     }\\n348: \\n349:     pub fn new(\\n350:         interner: I,\\n351:         trait_def_id: I::TraitId,\\n352:         args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\\n353:     ) -> Self {\\n354:         let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\\n355:         Self::new_from_args(interner, trait_def_id, args)\\n356:     }\\n357: \\n358:     pub fn erase_self_ty(interner: I, trait_ref: TraitRef<I>) -> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\\n359:         // Assert there is a Self.\\n360:         trait_ref.args.type_at(0);\\n361: \\n362:         ExistentialTraitRef {\\n363:             def_id: trait_ref.def_id,\\n364:             args: interner.mk_args(&trait_ref.args.as_slice()[1..]),\\n365:             _use_existential_trait_ref_new_instead: (),\\n366:         }\\n367:     }\\n368: \\n369:     /// Object types don't have a self type specified. Therefore, when\\n370:     /// we convert the principal trait-ref into a normal trait-ref,\\n371:     /// you must give *some* self type. A common choice is `mk_err()`\\n372:     /// or some placeholder type.\\n373:     pub fn with_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> TraitRef<I> {\\n374:         // otherwise the escaping vars would be captured by the binder\\n375:         // debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\\n376: \\n377:         TraitRef::new(interner, self.def_id, [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter()))\\n378:     }\\n379: }\\n380: \\n381: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialTraitRef<I>> {\\n382:     pub fn def_id(&self) -> I::TraitId {\\n383:         self.skip_binder().def_id\\n384:     }\\n385: \\n386:     /// Object types don't have a self type specified. Therefore, when\\n387:     /// we convert the principal trait-ref into a normal trait-ref,\\n388:     /// you must give *some* self type. A common choice is `mk_err()`\\n389:     /// or some placeholder type.\\n390:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, TraitRef<I>> {\\n391:         self.map_bound(|trait_ref| trait_ref.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty))\\n392:     }\\n393: }\\n394: \\n395: /// A `ProjectionPredicate` for an `ExistentialTraitRef`.\\n396: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\\n397: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n398: #[cfg_attr(\\n399:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n400:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n401: )]\\n402: pub struct ExistentialProjection<I: Interner> {\\n403:     pub def_id: I::DefId,\\n404:     pub args: I::GenericArgs,\\n405:     pub term: I::Term,\\n406: \\n407:     /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `ExistentialProjection`\\n408:     /// without using [`ExistentialProjection::new_from_args`].\\n409:     use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\\n410: }\\n411: \\n412: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialProjection<I> {}\\n413: \\n414: impl<I: Interner> ExistentialProjection<I> {\\n415:     pub fn new_from_args(\\n416:         interner: I,\\n417:         def_id: I::DefId,\\n418:         args: I::GenericArgs,\\n419:         term: I::Term,\\n420:     ) -> ExistentialProjection<I> {\\n421:         interner.debug_assert_existential_args_compatible(def_id, args);\\n422:         Self { def_id, args, term, use_existential_projection_new_instead: () }\\n423:     }\\n424: \\n425:     pub fn new(\\n426:         interner: I,\\n427:         def_id: I::DefId,\\n428:         args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\\n429:         term: I::Term,\\n430:     ) -> ExistentialProjection<I> {\\n431:         let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\\n432:         Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args, term)\\n433:     }\\n434: \\n435:     /// Extracts the underlying existential trait reference from this projection.\\n436:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `exists T. <T as Iterator>::Item == X`,\\n437:     /// then this function would return an `exists T. T: Iterator` existential trait\\n438:     /// reference.\\n439:     pub fn trait_ref(&self, interner: I) -> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\\n440:         let def_id = interner.parent(self.def_id);\\n441:         let args_count = interner.generics_of(def_id).count() - 1;\\n442:         let args = interner.mk_args(&self.args.as_slice()[..args_count]);\\n443:         ExistentialTraitRef::new_from_args(interner, def_id.try_into().unwrap(), args)\\n444:     }\\n445: \\n446:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\\n447:         // otherwise the escaping regions would be captured by the binders\\n448:         debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\\n449: \\n450:         ProjectionPredicate {\\n451:             projection_term: AliasTerm::new(\\n452:                 interner,\\n453:                 self.def_id,\\n454:                 [self_ty.into()].iter().chain(self.args.iter()),\\n455:             ),\\n456:             term: self.term,\\n457:         }\\n458:     }\\n459: \\n460:     pub fn erase_self_ty(interner: I, projection_predicate: ProjectionPredicate<I>) -> Self {\\n461:         // Assert there is a Self.\\n462:         projection_predicate.projection_term.args.type_at(0);\\n463: \\n464:         Self {\\n465:             def_id: projection_predicate.projection_term.def_id,\\n466:             args: interner.mk_args(&projection_predicate.projection_term.args.as_slice()[1..]),\\n467:             term: projection_predicate.term,\\n468:             use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\\n469:         }\\n470:     }\\n471: }\\n472: \\n473: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialProjection<I>> {\\n474:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, ProjectionPredicate<I>> {\\n475:         self.map_bound(|p| p.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty))\\n476:     }\\n477: \\n478:     pub fn item_def_id(&self) -> I::DefId {\\n479:         self.skip_binder().def_id\\n480:     }\\n481: }\\n482: \\n483: #[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Debug)]\\n484: #[cfg_attr(feature = \\\"nightly\\\", derive(Encodable, Decodable, HashStable_NoContext))]\\n485: pub enum AliasTermKind {\\n486:     /// A projection `<Type as Trait>::AssocType`.\\n487:     /// Can get normalized away if monomorphic enough.\\n488:     ProjectionTy,\\n489:     /// An associated type in an inherent `impl`\\n490:     InherentTy,\\n491:     /// An opaque type (usually from `impl Trait` in type aliases or function return types)\\n492:     /// Can only be normalized away in PostAnalysis mode or its defining scope.\\n493:     OpaqueTy,\\n494:     /// A free type alias that actually checks its trait bounds.\\n495:     /// Currently only used if the type alias references opaque types.\\n496:     /// Can always be normalized away.\\n497:     FreeTy,\\n498: \\n499:     /// An unevaluated anonymous constants.\\n500:     UnevaluatedConst,\\n501:     /// An unevaluated const coming from an associated const.\\n502:     ProjectionConst,\\n503:     /// A top level const item not part of a trait or impl.\\n504:     FreeConst,\\n505:     /// An associated const in an inherent `impl`\\n506:     InherentConst,\\n507: }\\n508: \\n509: impl AliasTermKind {\\n510:     pub fn descr(self) -> &'static str {\\n511:         match self {\\n512:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => \\\"associated type\\\",\\n513:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => \\\"associated const\\\",\\n514:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy => \\\"inherent associated type\\\",\\n515:             AliasTermKind::InherentConst => \\\"inherent associated const\\\",\\n516:             AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => \\\"opaque type\\\",\\n517:             AliasTermKind::FreeTy => \\\"type alias\\\",\\n518:             AliasTermKind::FreeConst => \\\"unevaluated constant\\\",\\n519:             AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst => \\\"unevaluated constant\\\",\\n520:         }\\n521:     }\\n522: \\n523:     pub fn is_type(self) -> bool {\\n524:         match self {\\n525:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\\n526:             | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\\n527:             | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\\n528:             | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => true,\\n529: \\n530:             AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\\n531:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\\n532:             | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n533:             | AliasTermKind::FreeConst => false,\\n534:         }\\n535:     }\\n536: }\\n537: \\n538: impl From<ty::AliasTyKind> for AliasTermKind {\\n539:     fn from(value: ty::AliasTyKind) -> Self {\\n540:         match value {\\n541:             ty::Projection => AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy,\\n542:             ty::Opaque => AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy,\\n543:             ty::Free => AliasTermKind::FreeTy,\\n544:             ty::Inherent => AliasTermKind::InherentTy,\\n545:         }\\n546:     }\\n547: }\\n548: \\n549: /// Represents the unprojected term of a projection goal.\\n550: ///\\n551: /// * For a projection, this would be `<Ty as Trait<...>>::N<...>`.\\n552: /// * For an inherent projection, this would be `Ty::N<...>`.\\n553: /// * For an opaque type, there is no explicit syntax.\\n554: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\\n555: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n556: #[cfg_attr(\\n557:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n558:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n559: )]\\n560: pub struct AliasTerm<I: Interner> {\\n561:     /// The parameters of the associated or opaque item.\\n562:     ///\\n563:     /// For a projection, these are the generic parameters for the trait and the\\n564:     /// GAT parameters, if there are any.\\n565:     ///\\n566:     /// For an inherent projection, they consist of the self type and the GAT parameters,\\n567:     /// if there are any.\\n568:     ///\\n569:     /// For RPIT the generic parameters are for the generics of the function,\\n570:     /// while for TAIT it is used for the generic parameters of the alias.\\n571:     pub args: I::GenericArgs,\\n572: \\n573:     /// The `DefId` of the `TraitItem` or `ImplItem` for the associated type `N` depending on whether\\n574:     /// this is a projection or an inherent projection or the `DefId` of the `OpaqueType` item if\\n575:     /// this is an opaque.\\n576:     ///\\n577:     /// During codegen, `interner.type_of(def_id)` can be used to get the type of the\\n578:     /// underlying type if the type is an opaque.\\n579:     ///\\n580:     /// Note that if this is an associated type, this is not the `DefId` of the\\n581:     /// `TraitRef` containing this associated type, which is in `interner.associated_item(def_id).container`,\\n582:     /// aka. `interner.parent(def_id)`.\\n583:     pub def_id: I::DefId,\\n584: \\n585:     /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `AliasTerm` without using [`AliasTerm::new_from_args`].\\n586:     #[derive_where(skip(Debug))]\\n587:     _use_alias_term_new_instead: (),\\n588: }\\n589: \\n590: impl<I: Interner> Eq for AliasTerm<I> {}\\n591: \\n592: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\\n593:     pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, def_id: I::DefId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> AliasTerm<I> {\\n594:         interner.debug_assert_args_compatible(def_id, args);\\n595:         AliasTerm { def_id, args, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\\n596:     }\\n597: \\n598:     pub fn new(\\n599:         interner: interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\\n604:         Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args)\\n605:     }\\n606: \\n607:     pub fn expect_ty(self, interner: I) -> ty::AliasTy<I> {\\n608:         match self.kind(interner) {\\n609:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\\n610:             | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\\n611:             | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\\n612:             | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => {}\\n613:             AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n614:             | AliasTermKind::FreeConst\\n615:             | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\\n616:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => {\\n617:                 panic!(\\\"Cannot turn `UnevaluatedConst` into `AliasTy`\\\")\\n618:             }\\n619:         }\\n620:         ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () }\\n621:     }\\n622: \\n623:     pub fn kind(self, interner: I) -> AliasTermKind {\\n624:         interner.alias_term_kind(self)\\n625:     }\\n626: \\n627:     pub fn to_term(self, interner: I) -> I::Term {\\n628:         match self.kind(interner) {\\n629:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n630:                 interner,\\n631:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Projection,\\n632:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n633:             )\\n634:             .into(),\\n635:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n636:                 interner,\\n637:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Inherent,\\n638:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n639:             )\\n640:             .into(),\\n641:             AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n642:                 interner,\\n643:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Opaque,\\n644:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n645:             )\\n646:             .into(),\\n647:             AliasTermKind::FreeTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n648:                 interner,\\n649:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Free,\\n650:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n651:             )\\n652:             .into(),\\n653:             AliasTermKind::FreeConst\\n654:             | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n655:             | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\\n656:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => I::Const::new_unevaluated(\\n657:                 interner,\\n658:                 ty::UnevaluatedConst::new(self.def_id.try_into().unwrap(), self.args),\\n659:             )\\n660:             .into(),\\n661:         }\\n662:     }\\n663: }\\n664: \\n665: /// The following methods work only with (trait) associated term projections.\\n666: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\\n667:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\\n668:         self.args.type_at(0)\\n669:     }\\n670: \\n671:     pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> Self {\\n672:         AliasTerm::new(\\n673:             interner,\\n674:             self.def_id,\\n675:             [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)),\\n676:         )\\n677:     }\\n678: \\n679:     pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\\n680:         assert!(\\n681:             matches!(\\n682:                 self.kind(interner),\\n683:                 AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\\n684:             ),\\n685:             \\\"expected a projection\\\"\\n686:         );\\n687:         interner.parent(self.def_id).try_into().unwrap()\\n688:     }\\n689: \\n690:     /// Extracts the underlying trait reference and own args from this projection.\\n691:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as StreamingIterator>::Item<'a>`,\\n692:     /// then this function would return a `T: StreamingIterator` trait reference and\\n693:     /// `['a]` as the own args.\\n694:     pub fn Extracts the underlying trait reference from this projection.\\n699:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as Iterator>::Item`,\\n700:     /// then this function would return a `T: Iterator` trait reference.\\n701:     ///\\n702:     /// WARNING: This will drop the args for generic associated types\\n703:     /// consider calling [Self::trait_ref_and_own_args] to get args.\\n712:     pub fn own_args(self, interner: I) -> I::GenericArgsSlice {\\n713:         self.trait_ref_and_own_args(interner).1\\n714:     }\\n715: }\\n716: \\n717: /// The following methods work only with inherent associated term projections.\\n718: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\\n719:     /// Transform the generic parameters to have the given `impl` args as the base and the GAT args on top of that.\\n720:     ///\\n721:     /// Does the following transformation:\\n722:     ///\\n723:     /// ```text\\n724:     /// [Self, P_0...P_m] -> [I_0...I_n, P_0...P_m]\\n725:     ///\\n726:     ///     I_i impl args\\n727:     ///     P_j GAT args\\n728:     /// ```\\n729:     pub fn rebase_inherent_args_onto_impl(\\n730:         self,\\n731:         impl_args: I::GenericArgs,\\n732:         interner: I,\\n733:     ) -> I::GenericArgs {\\n734:         debug_assert!(matches!(\\n735:             self.kind(interner),\\n736:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n737:         ));\\n738:         interner.mk_args_from_iter(impl_args.iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)))\\n739:     }\\n740: }\\n741: \\n742: impl<I: Interner> From<ty::AliasTy<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\\n743:     fn from(ty: ty::AliasTy<I>) -> Self {\\n744:         AliasTerm { args: ty.args, def_id: ty.def_id, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\\n745: ct.def.into(), _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\\n751:     }\\n752: }\\n753: \\n754: /// This kind of predicate has no *direct* correspondent in the\\n755: /// syntax, but it roughly corresponds to the syntactic forms:\\n756: ///\\n757: /// 1. `T: TraitRef<..., Item = Type>`\\n758: /// 2. `<T as TraitRef<...>>::Item == Type` (NYI)\\n759: ///\\n760: /// In particular, form #1 is \\\"desugared\\\" to the combination of a\\n761: /// normal trait predicate (`T: TraitRef<...>`) and one of these\\n762: /// predicates. Form #2 is a broader form in that it also permits\\n763: /// equality between arbitrary types. Processing an instance of\\n764: /// Form #2 eventually yields one of these `ProjectionPredicate`\\n765: /// instances to normalize the LHS.\\n766: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\\n767: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n768: #[cfg_attr(\\n769:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n770:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n771: )]\\n772: pub struct ProjectionPredicate<I: Interner> {\\n773:     pub projection_term: AliasTerm<I>,\\n774:     pub term: I::Term,\\n775: }\\n776: \\n777: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ProjectionPredicate<I> {}\\n778: \\n779: impl<I: Interner> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\\n780:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\\n781: }\\n799: \\n800: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ProjectionPredicate<I>> {\\n801:     /// Returns the `DefId` of the trait of the associated item being projected.\\n802:     #[inline]\\n803:     pub      // Ok to skip binder since trait `DefId` does not care about regions.\\n817:         self.skip_binder().projection_term.def_id\\n818:     }\\n819: }\\n820: \\n821: impl<I: Interner> fmt::Debug /// It is likely more useful to think of this as a function `normalizes_to(alias)`,\\n104:     /// whose return value is written into `term`.\\n105:     NormalizesTo(ty::NormalizesTo<I>),\\n106: \\n242: impl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::Clauses<TyCtxt<'tcx>> for ty::Clauses<'tcx> {}\\n243: \\n244: #[extension(pub trait ExistentialPredicateStableCmpExt<'tcx>)]\\n245: impl<'tcx> ExistentialPredicate<'tcx> {\\n246:     /// Compares via an ordering that will not change if modules are reordered or other changes are\\n247:     /// made to the tree. In particular, this ordering is preserved across incremental compilations.\\n248:     fn stable_cmp(&self, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>, other: &Self) -> Ordering {\\n249:         match (*self, *other) {\\n250:             (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => Ordering::Equal,\\n251:             (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref b)) => {\\n252:                 tcx.def_path_hash(a.def_id).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(b.def_id))\\n253:             }\\n254:             (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref b)) => {\\n255:                 tcx.def_path_hash(*a).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(*b))\\n256:             }\\n257:             (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\\n258:             (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => {\\n259:                 Ordering::Greater\\n260:             }\\n261:             (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\\n262:             (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_), _) => Ordering::Greater,\\n263:         }\\n264:     }\\n265: }\\n266: \\n267: pub type PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>;\\n268: \\n269: impl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::BoundExistentialPredicates<TyCtxt<'tcx>>\\n270:     for &'tcx ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>\\n271: {\\n272:     fn principal_def_id(self) -> Option<DefId> {\\n273:         self.principal_def_id()\\n274:     }\\n275: \\n276:     fn principal(self) -> Option<ty::PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>> {\\n277:         self.principal()\\n278:     }\\n279: \\n280:     fn auto_traits(self) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = DefId> {\\n281:         self.auto_traits()\\n282:     }\\n283: \\n284:     fn projection_bounds(\\n285:         self,\\n286:     ) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\\n287:         self.projection_bounds()\\n288:     }\\n289: }\\n290: \\n291: impl<'tcx> ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\\n292:     /// Returns the \\\"principal `DefId`\\\" of this set of existential predicates.\\n293:     ///\\n294:     /// A Rust trait object type consists (in addition to a lifetime bound)\\n295:     /// of a set of trait bounds, which are separated into any number\\n296:     /// of auto-trait bounds, and at most one non-auto-trait bound. The\\n297:     /// non-auto-trait bound is called the \\\"principal\\\" of the trait\\n298:     /// object.\\n299:     ///\\n300:     /// Only the principal can have methods or type parameters (because\\n301:     /// auto traits can have neither of them). This is important, because\\n302:     /// it means the auto traits can be treated as an unordered set (methods\\n303:     /// would force an order for the vtable, while relating traits with\\n304:     /// type parameters without knowing the order to relate them in is\\n305:     /// a rather non-trivial task).\\n306:     ///\\n307:     /// For example, in the trait object `dyn std::fmt::Debug + Sync`, the\\n308:     /// principal bound is `Some(std::fmt::Debug)`, while the auto-trait bounds\\n309:     /// are the set `{Sync}`.\\n310:     ///\\n311:     /// It is also possible to have a \\\"trivial\\\" trait object that\\n312:     /// consists only of auto traits, with no principal - for example,\\n313:     /// `dyn Send + Sync`. In that case, the set of auto-trait bounds\\n314:     /// is `{Send, Sync}`, while there is no principal. These trait objects\\n315:     /// have a \\\"trivial\\\" vtable consisting of just the size, alignment,\\n316:     /// and destructor.\\n317:     pub fn principal(&self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>> {\\n318:         self[0]\\n319:             .map_bound(|this| match this {\\n320:                 ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => Some(tr),\\n321:                 _ => None,\\n322:             })\\n323:             .transpose()\\n324:     }\\n325: \\n326:     pub fn principal_def_id(&self) -> Option<DefId> {\\n327:         self.principal().map(|trait_ref| trait_ref.skip_binder().def_id)\\n328:     }\\n329: \\n330:     #[inline]\\n331:     pub fn projection_bounds(\\n332:         &self,\\n333:     ) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\\n334:         self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| {\\n335:             predicate\\n336:                 .map_bound(|pred| match pred {\\n337:                     ExistentialPredicate::Projection(projection) => Some(projection),\\n338:                     _ => None,\\n339:                 })\\n340:                 .transpose()\\n341:         })\\n342:     }\\n343: \\n344:     #[inline]\\n345:     pub fn auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = DefId> {\\n346:         self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| match predicate.skip_binder() {\\n347:             ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => Some(did),\\n348:             _ => None,\\n349:         })\\n350:     }\\n351: \\n352:     pub fn without_auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\\n353:         self.iter().filter(|predicate| {\\n354:             !matches!(predicate.as_ref().skip_binder(), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_))\\n355:         })\\n356:     }\\n357: }\\n358: \\n359: pub type PolyTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitRef<'tcx>>;\\n360: pub type PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>;\\n361: pub type PolyExistentialProjection<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>;\\n362: \\n363: impl<'tcx> Clause<'tcx> {\\n364:     /// Performs a instantiation suitable for going from a\\n365:     /// poly-trait-ref to supertraits that must hold if that\\n366:     /// poly-trait-ref holds. This is slightly different from a normal\\n367:     /// instantiation in terms of what happens with bound regions. See\\n368:     /// lengthy comment below for details.\\n369:     pub fn instantiate_supertrait(\\n370:         self,\\n371:         tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>,\\n372:         trait_ref: ty::PolyTraitRef<'tcx>,\\n373:     ) -> Clause<'tcx> {\\n374:         // The interaction between HRTB and supertraits is not entirely\\n375:         // obvious. Let me walk you (and myself) through an example.\\n376:         //\\n377:         // Let's start with an easy case. Consider two traits:\\n378:         //\\n379:         //     trait Foo<'a>: Bar<'a,'a> { }\\n380:         //     trait Bar<'b,'c> { }\\n381:         //\\n382:         // Now, if we have a trait reference `for<'x> T: Foo<'x>`, then\\n383:         // we can deduce that `for<'x> T: Bar<'x,'x>`. Basically, if we\\n384:         // knew that `Foo<'x>` (for any 'x) then we also know that\\n385:         // `Bar<'x,'x>` (for any 'x). This more-or-less falls out from\\n386:         // normal instantiation.\\n387:         //\\n388:         // In terms of why this is sound, the idea is that whenever there\\n389:         // is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>`, it must show that `T:Bar<'a,'a>`\\n390:         // holds. So if there is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>` that applies to\\n391:         // all `'a`, then we must know that `T:Bar<'a,'a>` holds for all\\n392:         // `'a`.\\n393:         //\\n394:         // Another example to be careful of is this:\\n395:         //\\n396:         //     trait Foo1<'a>: for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b> { }\\n397:         //     trait Bar1<'b,'c> { }\\n398:         //\\n399:         // Here, if we have `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`, then what do we know?\\n400:         // The answer is that we know `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`. The\\n401:         // reason is similar to the previous example: any impl of\\n402:         // `T:Foo1<'x>` must show that `for<'b> T: Bar1<'x, 'b>`. So\\n403:         // basically we would want to collapse the bound lifetimes from\\n404:         // the input (`trait_ref`) and the supertraits.\\n405:         //\\n406:         // To achieve this in practice is fairly straightforward. Let's\\n407:         // consider the more complicated scenario:\\n408:         //\\n409:         // - We start out with `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`. In this case, `'x`\\n410:         //   has a De Bruijn index of 1. We want to produce `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`,\\n411:         //   where both `'x` and `'b` would have a DB index of 1.\\n412:         //   The instantiation from the input trait-ref is therefore going to be\\n413:         //   `'a => 'x` (where `'x` has a DB index of 1).\\n414:         // - The supertrait-ref is `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, where `'a` is an\\n415:         //   early-bound parameter and `'b` is a late-bound parameter with a\\n416:         //   DB index of 1.\\n417:         // - If we replace `'a` with `'x` from the input, it too will have\\n418:         //   a DB index of 1, and thus we'll have `for<'x,'b> Bar1<'x,'b>`\\n419:         //   just as we wanted.\\n420:         //\\n421:         // There is only one catch. If we just apply the instantiation `'a\\n422:         // => 'x` to `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, the instantiation code will\\n423:         // adjust the DB index because we instantiating into a binder (it\\n424:         // tries to be so smart...) resulting in `for<'x> for<'b>\\n425:         // Bar1<'x,'b>` (we have no syntax for this, so use your\\n426:         // imagination). Basically the 'x will have DB index of 2 and 'b\\n427:         // will have DB index of 1. Not quite what we want. So we apply\\n428:         // the instantiation to the *contents* of the trait reference,\\n429:         // rather than the trait reference itself (put another way, the\\n430:         // instantiation code expects equal binding levels in the values\\n431:         // from the instantiation and the value being instantiated into, and\\n432:         // this trick achieves that).\\n433: \\n434:         // Working through the second example:\\n435:         // trait_ref: for<'x> T: Foo1<'^0.0>; args: [T, '^0.0]\\n436:         // predicate: for<'b> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0>; args: [Self, 'a, '^0.0]\\n437:         // We want to end up with:\\n438:         //     for<'x, 'b> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\\n439:         // To do this:\\n440:         // 1) We must shift all bound vars in predicate by the length\\n441:         //    of trait ref's bound vars. So, we would end up with predicate like\\n442:         //    Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\\n443:         // 2) We can then apply the trait args to this, ending up with\\n444:         //    T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\\n445:         // 3) Finally, to create the final bound vars, we concatenate the bound\\n446:         //    vars of the trait ref with those of the predicate:\\n447:         //    ['x, 'b]\\n448:         let bound_pred = self.kind();\\n449:         let pred_bound_vars = bound_pred.bound_vars();\\n450:         let trait_bound_vars = trait_ref.bound_vars();\\n451:         // 1) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0> -> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\\n452:         let shifted_pred =\\n453:             tcx.shift_bound_var_indices(trait_bound_vars.len(), bound_pred.skip_binder());\\n454:         // 2) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1> -> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\\n455:         let new = EarlyBinder::bind(shifted_pred).instantiate(tcx, trait_ref.skip_binder().args);\\n456:         // 3) ['x] + ['b] -> ['x, 'b]\\n457:         let bound_vars =\\n458:             tcx.mk_bound_variable_kinds_from_iter(trait_bound_vars.iter().chain(pred_bound_vars));\\n459: \\n460:         // FIXME: Is it really perf sensitive to use reuse_or_mk_predicate here?\\n461:         tcx.reuse_or_mk_predicate(\\n462:             self.as_predicate(),\\n463:             ty::Binder::bind_with_vars(PredicateKind::Clause(new), bound_vars),\\n464:         )\\n465:         .expect_clause()\\n466:     }\\n467: }\\n468: \\n469: impl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, PredicateKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\\n470:     fn upcast_from(from: PredicateKind<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\\n471:         ty::Binder::dummy(from).upcast(tcx)\\n472:     }\\n473: }\\n474: \\n475: impl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\\n476:     fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\\n477:         tcx.mk_predicate(from)\\n478:     }\\n479: }\\n480: \\n481: impl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ClauseKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\\n482:     fn upcast_from(from: ClauseKind<'tcx>, tcx: impl<'tcx> Predicate<'tcx> {\\n637:     pub fn as_trait_clause(self) -> Option<PolyTraitPredicate<'tcx>> {\\n638:         let predicate = self.kind();\\n639:         match predicate.skip_binder() PredicateKind::Clause(ClauseKind::Projection(t)) => Some(predicate.rebind(t)),\\n649:             _ => None,\\n650:         }\\n651:     }\\n652: \\n653:     /// Matches a `PredicateKind::Clause` and turns it into a `Clause`, otherwise returns `None`.\\n654:     pub fn as_clause(self) -> Option<Clause<'tcx>> {\\n655:         match self.kind().skip_binder() {\\n656:             PredicateKind::Clause(..) => Some(self.expect_clause()),\\n657:             _ => None,\\n658:         }\\n659:     }\\n660: \\n661:     /// Assert that the predicate is a clause.\\n662:     pub fn self.sub_ty.print(p)?;\n    write!(p, \" <: \")?;\n    p.reset_type_limit();\n    self.super_ty.print(p)?;\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n## Analysis: Refactoring Strategy\n\n### Why This Change?\nThe current naming (`a` and `b`) is semantically opaque. The new naming (`sub_ty` for subtype, `super_ty` for supertype) immediately clarifies that:\n- `sub_ty <: super_ty` (subtype relation)\n- The `a_is_expected` flag controls which is presented as \"expected\" in diagnostics\n- This matches the documentation: \"Encodes that `a` must be a subtype of `b`\" — but now it's explicit in code\n\n### Affected Crates (9 total)\n1. `rustc_type_ir` — Core type IR definitions\n2. `rustc_public` — Public API layer\n3. `rustc_middle` — Mid-level type system\n4. `rustc_infer` — Type inference engine\n5. `rustc_type_ir` (relate module) — Type relation algorithms\n6. `rustc_hir_typeck` — HIR type checking\n7. `rustc_trait_selection` — Trait solver and error reporting\n8. `rustc_next_trait_solver` — Next-generation solver\n9. (Implicit) All downstream users get the new names via re-exports\n\n### Scope & Complexity\n- **Total files to modify**: 13 core files + supporting infrastructure\n- **Line count affected**: ~25 distinct code sections\n- **Breaking changes**: None within the same crate (all internal to Rust compiler)\n- **Cross-crate impact**: Minimal — mostly internal compiler-only APIs\n- **Test coverage**: Existing tests validate functionality; naming changes don't alter logic\n\n### Verification Approach\n1. **Compilation check**: `cargo check` on affected crates\n2. **Type safety**: Rust compiler ensures all pattern matches are exhaustive\n3. **Functional tests**: Existing test suite validates behavior\n4. **Grep verification**: Search for residual `SubtypePredicate` (should only find in Subtype variant name or comments)\n\n---\n\n## Implementation Notes\n\n### Field Renaming Rationale\n- `a` → `sub_ty`: The type that must be a **sub**type\n- `b` → `super_ty`: The type that must be a **super**type\n- `a_is_expected` → (unchanged): Flag controlling diagnostic presentation, distinct semantic meaning\n\n### Variance-Based Field Swapping\nFiles `type_relating.rs` and `solver_relating.rs` have special handling:\n```rust\n// Contravariant case swaps the fields:\nty::SubtypeRelation { a_is_expected: false, sub_ty: b, super_ty: a }\n```\nThis is intentional and correct — contravariance reverses the subtype relationship. Verify comments are preserved.\n\n### Enum Variant Name\nThe `PredicateKind::Subtype` variant **name stays as-is** (only the struct type inside changes). This is intentional because:\n- The variant name `Subtype` is a semantic label (not a struct name)\n- Only the type parameter changes: `PredicateKind::Subtype(SubtypeRelation<I>)` vs `PredicateKind::Subtype(SubtypePredicate<I>)`\n\n---\n\n## Files Requiring Changes - Complete Checklist\n\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_type_ir/src/predicate.rs` (Struct def + impl, 1 occurrence)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_public/src/ty.rs` (Public struct def, 1 occurrence)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_middle/src/ty/predicate.rs` (Type alias, 2 occurrences)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_type_ir/src/predicate_kind.rs` (Variant type, 1 occurrence)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_infer/src/infer/mod.rs` (5 occurrences: construction + field access + destructuring)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_type_ir/src/flags.rs` (1 occurrence: pattern match)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_hir_typeck/src/fallback.rs` (1 occurrence: pattern match)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_trait_selection/src/error_reporting/traits/overflow.rs` (1 occurrence: pattern match)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_trait_selection/src/error_reporting/traits/ambiguity.rs` (1 occurrence: destructuring)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_next_trait_solver/src/solve/mod.rs` (3 occurrences: construction + field access)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_type_ir/src/relate/solver_relating.rs` (2 occurrences: construction with variance)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_infer/src/infer/relate/type_relating.rs` (2 occurrences: construction with variance)\n- [ ] `compiler/rustc_middle/src/ty/print/pretty.rs` (1 occurrence: field access in printing)\n\n**Total: 13 files, ~25 code sections**\n\n---\n\n## Expected Outcome\n\nAfter this refactoring:\n1. \\n293: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialPredicate<I>> {\\n294:     /// Given an existential predicate like `?Self: PartialEq<u32>` (e.g., derived from `dyn PartialEq<u32>`),\\n295:     /// and a concrete type `self_ty`, returns a full predicate where the existentially quantified variable `?Self`\\n296:     /// has been replaced with `self_ty` (e.g., `self_ty: PartialEq<u32>`, in our example).\\n297:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> I::Clause {\\n298:         match self.skip_binder() {\\n299:             ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => self.rebind(tr).with_self_ty(cx, self_ty).upcast(cx),\\n300:             ExistentialPredicate::Projection(p) => {\\n301:                 self.rebind(p.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty)).upcast(cx)\\n302:             }\\n303:             ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => {\\n304:                 let generics = cx.generics_of(did.into());\\n305:                 let trait_ref = if generics.count() == 1         // new error args for the missing generics.\\n310:                     let err_args =\\n311:                         GenericArgs::extend_with_error(cx, did.into(), &[self_ty.into()]);\\n312: }\\n318: }\\n319: \\n320: /// An existential reference to a trait, where `Self` is erased.\\n321: /// For example, the trait object `Trait<'a, 'b, X, Y>` is:\\n322: /// ```ignore (illustrative)\\n323: /// exists T. Therefore, when\\n370:     /// we convert the principal trait-ref into a normal trait-ref,\\n371:     /// you must give *some* self type. A common choice is `mk_err()`\\n372:     /// or some placeholder type.\\n373:     pub fn with_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> TraitRef<I> {\\n374:         // otherwise the escaping vars would be captured by the binder\\n375:         // debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\\n376: \\n377:         TraitRef::new(interner, self.def_id, [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter()))\\n378:     }\\n379: }\\n380: \\n381: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialTraitRef<I>> {\\n382:     pub fn def_id(&self) -> I::TraitId {\\n383:         self.skip_binder().def_id\\n384:     }\\n385: \\n386:     /// Object types don't have a self type specified. Therefore, when\\n387:     /// we convert the principal trait-ref into a normal trait-ref,\\n388:     /// you must give *some* self type. A common choice is `mk_err()`\\n389:     /// or some placeholder type.\\n390:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, TraitRef<I>> {\\n391:         self.map_bound(|trait_ref| trait_ref.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty))\\n392:     }\\n393: }\\n394: \\n395: /// A `ProjectionPredicate` for an `ExistentialTraitRef`.\\n396: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\\n397: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n398: #[cfg_attr(\\n399:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n400:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n401: )]\\n402: pub struct ExistentialProjection<I: Interner> {\\n403:     pub def_id: I::DefId,\\n404:     pub args: I::GenericArgs,\\n405:     pub term: I::Term,\\n406: \\n407:     /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `ExistentialProjection`\\n408:     /// without using [`ExistentialProjection::new_from_args`].\\n409:     use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\\n410: }\\n411: \\n412: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialProjection<I> {}\\n413: \\n414: impl<I: Interner> ExistentialProjection<I> {\\n415:     pub fn new_from_args(\\n416:         interner: I,\\n417:         def_id: I::DefId,\\n418:         args: I::GenericArgs,\\n419:         term: I::Term,\\n420:     ) -> ExistentialProjection<I> {\\n421:         interner.debug_assert_existential_args_compatible(def_id, args);\\n422:         Self { def_id, args, term, use_existential_projection_new_instead: () }\\n423:     }\\n424: \\n425:     pub fn new(\\n426:         interner: I,\\n427:         def_id: I::DefId,\\n428:         args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\\n429:         term: I::Term,\\n430:     ) -> ExistentialProjection<I> {\\n431:         let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\\n432:         Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args, term)\\n433:     }\\n434: \\n435:     /// Extracts the underlying existential trait reference from this projection.\\n436:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `exists T. <T as Iterator>::Item == X`,\\n437:     /// then this function would return an `exists T. T: Iterator` existential trait\\n438:     /// reference.\\n439:     pub fn trait_ref(&self, interner: I) -> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\\n440:         let def_id = interner.parent(self.def_id);\\n441:         let args_count = interner.generics_of(def_id).count() - 1;\\n442:         let args = interner.mk_args(&self.args.as_slice()[..args_count]);\\n443:         ExistentialTraitRef::new_from_args(interner, def_id.try_into().unwrap(), args)\\n444:     }\\n445: \\n446:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\\n447:         // otherwise the escaping regions would be captured by the binders\\n448:         debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\\n449: \\n450:         ProjectionPredicate {\\n451:             projection_term: AliasTerm::new(\\n452:                 interner,\\n453:                 self.def_id,\\n454:                 [self_ty.into()].iter().chain(self.args.iter()),\\n455:             ),\\n456:             term: self.term,\\n457:         }\\n458:     }\\n459: \\n460:     pub fn erase_self_ty(interner: I, projection_predicate: ProjectionPredicate<I>) -> Self {\\n461:         // Assert there is a Self.\\n462:         projection_predicate.projection_term.args.type_at(0);\\n463: \\n464:         Self {\\n465:             def_id: projection_predicate.projection_term.def_id,\\n466:             args: interner.mk_args(&projection_predicate.projection_term.args.as_slice()[1..]),\\n467:             term: projection_predicate.term,\\n468:             use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\\n469:         }\\n470:     }\\n471: }\\n472: \\n473: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialProjection<I>> {\\n474:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, ProjectionPredicate<I>> {\\n475:         self.map_bound(|p| p.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty))\\n476:     }\\n477: \\n478:     pub fn item_def_id(&self) -> I::DefId {\\n479:         self.skip_binder().def_id\\n480:     }\\n481: }\\n482: \\n483: #[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Debug)]\\n484: #[cfg_attr(feature = \\\"nightly\\\", derive(Encodable, Decodable, HashStable_NoContext))]\\n485: pub enum AliasTermKind {\\n486:     /// A projection `<Type as Trait>::AssocType`.\\n487:     /// Can get normalized away if monomorphic enough.\\n488:     ProjectionTy,\\n489:     /// An associated type in an inherent `impl`\\n490:     InherentTy,\\n491:     /// An opaque type (usually from `impl Trait` in type aliases or function return types)\\n492:     /// Can only be normalized away in PostAnalysis mode or its defining scope.\\n493:     OpaqueTy,\\n494:     /// A free type alias that actually checks its trait bounds.\\n495:     /// Currently only used if the type alias references opaque types.\\n496:     /// Can always be normalized away.\\n497:     FreeTy,\\n498: \\n499:     /// An unevaluated anonymous constants.\\n500:     UnevaluatedConst,\\n501:     /// An unevaluated const coming from an associated const.\\n502:     ProjectionConst,\\n503:     /// A top level const item not part of a trait or impl.\\n504:     FreeConst,\\n505:     /// An associated const in an inherent `impl`\\n506:     InherentConst,\\n507: }\\n508: \\n509: impl AliasTermKind {\\n510:     pub fn descr(self) -> &'static str {\\n511:         match self {\\n512:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => \\\"associated type\\\",\\n513:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => \\\"associated const\\\",\\n514:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy => \\\"inherent associated type\\\",\\n515:             AliasTermKind::InherentConst => \\\"inherent associated const\\\",\\n516:             AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => \\\"opaque type\\\",\\n517:             AliasTermKind::FreeTy => \\\"type alias\\\",\\n518:             AliasTermKind::FreeConst => \\\"unevaluated constant\\\",\\n519:             AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst => \\\"unevaluated constant\\\",\\n520:         }\\n521:     }\\n522: \\n523:     pub fn is_type(self) -> bool {\\n524:         match self {\\n525:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\\n526:             | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\\n527:             | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\\n528:             | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => true,\\n529: \\n530:             AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\\n531:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\\n532:             | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n533:             | AliasTermKind::FreeConst => false,\\n534:         }\\n535:     }\\n536: }\\n537: \\n538: impl From<ty::AliasTyKind> for AliasTermKind {\\n539:     fn from(value: ty::AliasTyKind) -> Self {\\n540:         match value {\\n541:             ty::Projection => AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy,\\n542:             ty::Opaque => AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy,\\n543:             ty::Free => AliasTermKind::FreeTy,\\n544:             ty::Inherent => AliasTermKind::InherentTy,\\n545:         }\\n546:     }\\n547: }\\n548: \\n549: /// Represents the unprojected term of a projection goal.\\n550: ///\\n551: /// * For a projection, this would be `<Ty as Trait<...>>::N<...>`.\\n552: /// * For an inherent projection, this would be `Ty::N<...>`.\\n553: /// * For an opaque type, there is no explicit syntax.\\n554: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\\n555: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n556: #[cfg_attr(\\n557:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n558:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n559: )]\\n560: pub struct AliasTerm<I: Interner> {\\n561:     /// The parameters of the associated or opaque item.\\n562:     ///\\n563:     /// For a projection, these are the generic parameters for the trait and the\\n564:     /// GAT parameters, if there are any.\\n565:     ///\\n566:     /// For an inherent projection, they consist of the self type and the GAT parameters,\\n567:     /// if there are any.\\n568:     ///\\n569:     /// For RPIT the generic parameters are for the generics of the function,\\n570:     /// while for TAIT it is used for the generic parameters of the alias.\\n571:     pub args: I::GenericArgs,\\n572: \\n573:     /// The `DefId` of the `TraitItem` or `ImplItem` for the associated type `N` depending on whether\\n574:     /// this is a projection or an inherent projection or the `DefId` of the `OpaqueType` item if\\n575:     /// this is an opaque.\\n576:     ///\\n577:     /// During codegen, `interner.type_of(def_id)` can be used to get the type of the\\n578:     /// underlying type if the type is an opaque.\\n579:     ///\\n580:     /// Note that if this is an associated type, this is not the `DefId` of the\\n581:     /// `TraitRef` containing this associated type, which is in `interner.associated_item(def_id).container`,\\n582:     /// aka. `interner.parent(def_id)`.\\n583:     pub def_id: I::DefId,\\n584: \\n585:     /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `AliasTerm` without using [`AliasTerm::new_from_args`].\\n586:     #[derive_where(skip(Debug))]\\n587:     _use_alias_term_new_instead: (),\\n588: }\\n589: \\n590: impl<I: Interner> Eq for AliasTerm<I> {}\\n591: \\n592: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\\n593:     pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, def_id: I::DefId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> AliasTerm<I> {\\n594:         interner.debug_assert_args_compatible(def_id, args);\\n595:         AliasTerm { def_id, args, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\\n596:     }\\n597: \\n598:     pub fn new(\\n599:         interner: I,\\n600:         def_id: I::DefId,\\n601:         args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\\n602:     ) -> AliasTerm<I> {\\n603:         let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\\n604:         Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args)\\n605:     }\\n606: \\n607:     pub fn expect_ty(self, interner: I) -> ty::AliasTy<I> {\\n608:         match self.kind(interner) {\\n609:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\\n610:             | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\\n611:             | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\\n612:             | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => {}\\n613:             AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n614:             | AliasTermKind::FreeConst\\n615:             | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\\n616:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => {\\n617:                 panic!(\\\"Cannot turn `UnevaluatedConst` into `AliasTy`\\\")\\n618:             }\\n619:         }\\n620:         ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () }\\n621:     }\\n622: \\n623:     pub fn kind(self, interner: I) -> AliasTermKind {\\n624:         interner.alias_term_kind(self)\\n625:     }\\n626: \\n627:     pub fn to_term(self, interner: I) -> I::Term {\\n628:         match self.kind(interner) {\\n629:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n630:                 interner,\\n631:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Projection,\\n632:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n633:             )\\n634:             .into(),\\n635:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n636:                 interner,\\n637:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Inherent,\\n638:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n639:             )\\n640:             .into(),\\n641:             AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n642:                 interner,\\n643:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Opaque,\\n644:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n645:             )\\n646:             .into(),\\n647:             AliasTermKind::FreeTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n648:                 interner,\\n649:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Free,\\n650:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n651:             )\\n652:             .into(),\\n653:             AliasTermKind::FreeConst\\n654:             | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n655:             | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\\n656:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => I::Const::new_unevaluated(\\n657:                 interner,\\n658:                 ty::UnevaluatedConst::new(self.def_id.try_into().unwrap(), self.args),\\n659:             )\\n660:             .into(),\\n661:         }\\n662:     }\\n663: }\\n664: \\n665: /// The following methods work only with (trait) associated term projections.\\n666: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\\n667:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\\n668:         self.args.type_at(0)\\n669:     }\\n670: \\n671:     pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> Self {\\n672:         AliasTerm::new(\\n673:             interner,\\n674:             self.def_id,\\n675:             [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)),\\n676:         )\\n677:     }\\n678: \\n679:     pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\\n680:         assert!(\\n681:             matches!(\\n682:                 self.kind(interner),\\n683:                 AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\\n684:             ),\\n685:             \\\"expected a projection\\\"\\n686:         );\\n687:         interner.parent(self.def_id).try_into().unwrap()\\n688:     }\\n689: \\n690:     /// Extracts the underlying trait reference and own args from this projection.\\n691:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as StreamingIterator>::Item<'a>`,\\n692:     /// then this function would return a `T: StreamingIterator` trait reference and\\n693:     /// `['a]` as the own args.\\n694:     pub fn trait_ref_and_own_args(self, interner: I) -> (TraitRef<I>, I::GenericArgsSlice) {\\n695:         interner.trait_ref_and_own_args_for_alias(self.def_id, self.args)\\n696:     }\\n697: \\n698:     /// Extracts the underlying trait reference from this projection.\\n699:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as Iterator>::Item`,\\n700:     /// then this function would return a `T: Iterator` trait reference.\\n701:     ///\\n702:     /// WARNING: This will drop the args for generic associated types\\n703:     /// consider calling [Self::trait_ref_and_own_args] to get those\\n704:     /// as well.\\n705:     pub fn trait_ref(self, interner: I) -> TraitRef<I> {\\n706:         self.trait_ref_and_own_args(interner).0\\n707:     }\\n708: \\n709:     /// Extract the own args from this projection.\\n710:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as StreamingIterator>::Item<'a>`,\\n711:     /// then this function would return the slice `['a]` as the own args.\\n712:     pub fn own_args(self, interner: I) -> I::GenericArgsSlice {\\n713:         self.trait_ref_and_own_args(interner).1\\n714:     }\\n715: }\\n716: \\n717: /// The following methods work only with inherent associated term projections.\\n718: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\\n719:     /// Transform the generic parameters to have the given `impl` args as the base and the GAT args on top of that.\\n720:     ///\\n721:     /// Does the following transformation:\\n722:     ///\\n723:     /// ```text\\n724:     /// [Self, P_0...P_m] -> [I_0...I_n, P_0...P_m]\\n725:     ///\\n726:     ///     I_i impl args\\n727:     ///     P_j GAT args\\n728:     /// ```\\n729:     pub fn rebase_inherent_args_onto_impl(\\n730:         self,\\n731:         impl_args: I::GenericArgs,\\n732:         interner: I,\\n733:     ) -> I::GenericArgs {\\n734:         debug_assert!(matches!(\\n735:             self.kind(interner),\\n736:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n737:         ));\\n738:         interner.mk_args_from_iter(impl_args.iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)))\\n739:     }\\n740: }\\n741: \\n742: impl<I: Interner> From<ty::AliasTy<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\\n743:     fn from(ty: ty::AliasTy<I>) -> Self {\\n744:         AliasTerm { args: ty.args, def_id: ty.def_id, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\\n745:     }\\n746: }\\n747: \\n748: impl<I: Interner> From<ty::UnevaluatedConst<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\\n749:     fn from(ct: ty::UnevaluatedConst<I>) -> Self {\\n750:         AliasTerm { args: ct.args, def_id: ct.def.into(), _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\\n751:     }\\n752: }\\n753: \\n754: /// This kind of predicate has no *direct* correspondent in the\\n755: /// syntax, but it roughly corresponds to the syntactic forms:\\n756: ///\\n757: /// 1. `T: TraitRef<..., Item = Type>`\\n758: /// 2. `<T as TraitRef<...>>::Item == Type` (NYI)\\n759: ///\\n760: /// In particular, form #1 is \\\"desugared\\\" to the combination of a\\n761: /// normal trait predicate (`T: TraitRef<...>`) and one of these\\n762: /// predicates. Form #2 is a broader form in that it also permits\\n763: /// equality between arbitrary types. Processing an instance of\\n764: /// Form #2 eventually yields one of these `ProjectionPredicate`\\n765: /// instances to normalize the LHS.\\n766: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\\n767: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n768: #[cfg_attr(\\n769:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n770:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n771: )]\\n772: pub struct ProjectionPredicate<I: Interner> {\\n773:     pub projection_term: AliasTerm<I>,\\n774:     pub term: I::Term,\\n775: }\\n776: \\n777: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ProjectionPredicate<I> {}\\n778: \\n779: impl<I: Interner> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\\n780:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\\n781:         self.projection_term.self_ty()\\n782:     }\\n783: \\n784:     pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\\n785:         Self {\\n786:             projection_term: self.projection_term.with_replaced_self_ty(interner, self_ty),\\n787:             ..self\\n788:         }\\n789:     }\\n790: \\n791:     pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\\n792:         self.projection_term.trait_def_id(interner)\\n793:     }\\n794: \\n795:     pub fn def_id(self) -> I::DefId {\\n796:         self.projection_term.def_id\\n797:     }\\n798: }\\n799: \\n800: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ProjectionPredicate<I>> {\\n801:     /// Returns the `DefId` of the trait of the associated item being projected.\\n802:     #[inline]\\n803:     pub fn trait_def_id(&self, cx: I) -> I::TraitId {\\n804:         self.skip_binder().projection_term.trait_def_id(cx)\\n805:     }\\n806: \\n807:     pub fn term(&self) -> ty::Binder<I, I::Term> {\\n808:         self.map_bound(|predicate| predicate.term)\\n809:     }\\n810: \\n811:     /// The `DefId` of the `TraitItem` for the associated type.\\n812:     ///\\n813:     /// Note that this is not the `DefId` of the `TraitRef` containing this\\n814:     /// associated type, which is in `tcx.associated_item(projection_def_id()).container`.\\n815:     pub fn item_def_id(&self) -> I::DefId {\\n816:         // Ok to skip binder since trait `DefId` does not care about regions.\\n817:         self.skip_binder().projection_term.def_id\\n818:     }\\n819: }\\n820: \\n821: impl<I: Interner> fmt::Debug for ProjectionPredicate<I> {\\n822:     fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\\n823:         write!(f, \\\"ProjectionPredicate({:?}, {:?})\\\", self.projection_term, self.term)\\n824:     }\\n825: }\\n826: \\n827: /// Used by the new solver to normalize an alias. This always expects the `term` to\\n828: /// be an unconstrained inference variable which is used as the output.\\n829: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\\n830: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n831: #[cfg_attr(\\n832:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n833:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n834: )]\\n835: pub struct NormalizesTo<I: Interner> {\\n836:     pub alias: AliasTerm<I>,\\n837:     pub term: I::Term,\\n838: }\\n839: \\n840: impl<I: Interner> Eq for NormalizesTo<I> {}\\n841: \\n842: impl<I: Interner> NormalizesTo<I> {\\n843:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\\n844:         self.alias.self_ty()\\n845:     }\\n846: \\n847:     pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> NormalizesTo<I> {\\n848:         Self { alias: self.alias.with_replaced_self_ty(interner, self_ty), ..self }\\n849:     }\\n850: \\n851:     pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\\n852:         self.alias.trait_def_id(interner)\\n853:     }\\n854: \\n855:     pub fn def_id(self) -> I::DefId {\\n856:         self.alias.def_id\\n857:     }\\n858: }\\n859: \\n860: impl<I: Interner> fmt::Debug for NormalizesTo<I> {\\n861:     fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\\n862:         write!(f, \\\"NormalizesTo({:?}, {:?})\\\", self.alias, self.term)\\n863:     }\\n864: }\\n865: \\n866: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\\n867: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n868: #[cfg_attr(\\n869:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n870:     derive(Encodable_NoContext, Decodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n871: )]\\n872: pub struct HostEffectPredicate<I: Interner> {\\n873:     pub trait_ref: ty::TraitRef<I>,\\n874:     pub constness: BoundConstness,\\n875: }\\n876: \\n877: impl<I: Interner> Eq for HostEffectPredicate<I> {}\\n878: \\n879: impl<I: Interner> HostEffectPredicate<I> {\\n880:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\\n881:         self.trait_ref.self_ty()\\n882:     }\\n883: \\n884:     pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> Self {\\n885:         Self { trait_ref: self.trait_ref.with_replaced_self_ty(interner, self_ty), ..self }\\n886:     }\\n887: \\n888:     pub fn def_id(self) -> I::TraitId {\\n889:         self.trait_ref.def_id\\n890:     }\\n891: }\\n892: \\n893: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, HostEffectPredicate<I>> {\\n894:     pub fn def_id(self) -> I::TraitId {\\n895:         // Ok to skip binder since trait `DefId` does not care about regions.\\n896:         self.skip_binder().def_id()\\n897:     }\\n898: \\n899:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> ty::Binder<I, I::Ty> {\\n900:         self.map_bound(|trait_ref| trait_ref.self_ty())\\n901:     }\\n902: \\n903:     #[inline]\\n904:     pub fn constness(self) -> BoundConstness {\\n905:         self.skip_binder().constness\\n906:     }\\n907: }\\n908: \\n909: /// Encodes that `a` must be a subtype of `b`. The `a_is_expected` flag indicates\\n910: /// whether the `a` type is the type that we should label as \\\"expected\\\" when\\n911: /// presenting user diagnostics.\\n912: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\\n913: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n914: #[cfg_attr(\\n915:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n916: \\n293: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialPredicate<I>> {\\n294:     /// Given an existential predicate like `?Self: PartialEq<u32>` (e.g., derived from `dyn PartialEq<u32>`),\\n295:     /// and a concrete type `self_ty`, returns a full predicate where the existentially quantified variable `?Self`\\n296:     /// has been replaced with `self_ty` (e.g., `self_ty: PartialEq<u32>`, in our example).\\n297:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> I::Clause {\\n298:         match self.skip_binder() {\\n299:             ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => self.rebind(tr).with_self_ty(cx, self_ty).upcast(cx),\\n300:             ExistentialPredicate::Projection(p) => {\\n301:                 self.rebind(p.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty)).upcast(cx)\\n302:             }\\n303:             ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => {\\n304:                 let generics = cx.generics_of(did.into());\\n305:                 let trait_ref = if generics.count() == 1 {\\n306:                     ty::TraitRef::new(cx, did, [self_ty])\\n307:                 } else {\\n308:                     // If this is an ill-formed auto trait, then synthesize\\n309:                     // new error args for the missing generics.\\n310:                     let err_args =\\n311:                         GenericArgs::extend_with_error(cx, did.into(), &[self_ty.into()]);\\n312:                     ty::TraitRef::new_from_args(cx, did, err_args)\\n313:                 };\\n314:                 self.rebind(trait_ref).upcast(cx)\\n315:             }\\n316:         }\\n317: /// exists T. Therefore, when\\n370:     /// we convert the principal trait-ref into a normal trait-ref,\\n371:     /// you must give *some* self type. A common choice is `mk_err()`\\n372:     /// or some placeholder type.\\n373:     pub fn with_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> TraitRef<I> {\\n374:         // otherwise the escaping vars would be captured by the binder\\n375:         // debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\\n376: \\n377:         TraitRef::new(interner, self.def_id, [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter()))\\n378:     }\\n379: }\\n380: \\n381: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialTraitRef<I>> {\\n382:     pub fn def_id(&self) -> I::TraitId {\\n383:         self.skip_binder().def_id\\n384:     }\\n385: \\n386:     /// Object types don't have a self type specified.
