T: Iterator` existential trait\\n438:     /// reference.\\n439:     pub fn trait_ref(&self, interner: I) -> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\\n440:         let def_id = interner.parent(self.def_id);\\n441:         let args_count = interner.generics_of(def_id).count() - 1;\\n442:         let args = interner.mk_args(&self.args.as_slice()[..args_count]);\\n443:         ExistentialTraitRef::new_from_args(interner, def_id.try_into().unwrap(), args)\\n444:     }\\n445: \\n446:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\\n447:         // otherwise the escaping regions would be captured by the binders\\n448:         debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\\n449: \\n450:         ProjectionPredicate {\\n451:             projection_term: AliasTerm::new(\\n452:                 interner,\\n453:                 self.def_id,\\n454:                 [self_ty.into()].iter().chain(self.args.iter()),\\n455:             ),\\n456:             term: self.term,\\n457:         }\\n458:     }\\n459: \\n460:     pub fn erase_self_ty(interner: I, projection_predicate: ProjectionPredicate<I>) -> Self {\\n461:         // Assert there is a Self.\\n462:         projection_predicate.projection_term.args.type_at(0);\\n463: \\n464:         Self {\\n465:             def_id: projection_predicate.projection_term.def_id,\\n466:             args: interner.mk_args(&projection_predicate.projection_term.args.as_slice()[1..]),\\n467:             term: projection_predicate.term,\\n468:             use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\\n469:         }\\n470:     }\\n471: }\\n472: \\n473: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialProjection<I>> {\\n474:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, ProjectionPredicate<I>> {\\n475:         self.map_bound(|p| p.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty))\\n476:     }\\n477: \\n478:     pub fn item_def_id(&self) -> I::DefId {\\n479:         self.skip_binder().def_id\\n480:     }\\n481: }\\n482: \\n483: #[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Debug)]\\n484: #[cfg_attr(feature = \\\"nightly\\\", derive(Encodable, Decodable, HashStable_NoContext))]\\n485: pub enum AliasTermKind {\\n486:     /// A projection `<Type as Trait>::AssocType`.\\n487:     /// Can get normalized away if monomorphic enough.\\n488:     ProjectionTy,\\n489:     /// An associated type in an inherent `impl`\\n490:     InherentTy,\\n491:     /// An opaque type (usually from `impl Trait` in type aliases or function return types)\\n492:     /// Can only be normalized away in PostAnalysis mode or its defining scope.\\n493:     OpaqueTy,\\n494:     /// A free type alias that actually checks its trait bounds.\\n495:     /// Currently only used if the type alias references opaque types.\\n496:     /// Can always be normalized away.\\n497:     FreeTy,\\n498: \\n499:     /// An unevaluated anonymous constants.\\n500:     UnevaluatedConst,\\n501:     /// An unevaluated const coming from an associated const.\\n502:     ProjectionConst,\\n503:     /// A top level const item not part of a trait or impl.\\n504:     FreeConst,\\n505:     /// An associated const in an inherent `impl`\\n506:     InherentConst,\\n507: }\\n508: \\n509: impl AliasTermKind {\\n510:     pub fn descr(self) -> &'static str {\\n511:         match self {\\n512:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => \\\"associated type\\\",\\n513:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => \\\"associated const\\\",\\n514:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy => \\\"inherent associated type\\\",\\n515:             AliasTermKind::InherentConst => \\\"inherent associated const\\\",\\n516:             AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => \\\"opaque type\\\",\\n517:             AliasTermKind::FreeTy => \\\"type alias\\\",\\n518:             AliasTermKind::FreeConst => \\\"unevaluated constant\\\",\\n519:             AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst => \\\"unevaluated constant\\\",\\n520:         }\\n521:     }\\n522: \\n523:     pub fn is_type(self) -> bool {\\n524:         match self {\\n525:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\\n526:             | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\\n527:             | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\\n528:             | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => true,\\n529: \\n530:             AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\\n531:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\\n532:             | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n533:             | AliasTermKind::FreeConst => false,\\n534:         }\\n535:     }\\n536: }\\n537: \\n538: impl From<ty::AliasTyKind> for AliasTermKind {\\n539:     fn from(value: ty::AliasTyKind) -> Self {\\n540:         match value {\\n541:             ty::Projection => AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy,\\n542:             ty::Opaque => AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy,\\n543:             ty::Free => AliasTermKind::FreeTy,\\n544:             ty::Inherent => AliasTermKind::InherentTy,\\n545:         }\\n546:     }\\n547: }\\n548: \\n549: /// Represents the unprojected term of a projection goal.\\n550: ///\\n551: /// * For a projection, this would be `<Ty as Trait<...>>::N<...>`.\\n552: /// * For an inherent projection, this would be `Ty::N<...>`.\\n553: /// * For an opaque type, there is no explicit syntax.\\n554: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\\n555: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n556: #[cfg_attr(\\n557:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n558:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n559: )]\\n560: pub struct AliasTerm<I: Interner> {\\n561:     /// The parameters of the associated or opaque item.\\n562:     ///\\n563:     /// For a projection, these are the generic parameters for the trait and the\\n564:     /// GAT parameters, if there are any.\\n565:     ///\\n566:     /// For an inherent projection, they consist of the self type and the GAT parameters,\\n567:     /// if there are any.\\n568:     ///\\n569:     /// For RPIT the generic parameters are for the generics of the function,\\n570:     /// while for TAIT it is used for the generic parameters of the alias.\\n571:     pub args: I::GenericArgs,\\n572: \\n573:     /// The `DefId` of the `TraitItem` or `ImplItem` for the associated type `N` depending on whether\\n574:     /// this is a projection or an inherent projection or the `DefId` of the `OpaqueType` item if\\n575:     /// this is an opaque.\\n576:     ///\\n577:     /// During codegen, `interner.type_of(def_id)` can be used to get the type of the\\n578:     /// underlying type if the type is an opaque.\\n579:     ///\\n580:     /// Note that if this is an associated type, this is not the `DefId` of the\\n581:     /// `TraitRef` containing this associated type, which is in `interner.associated_item(def_id).container`,\\n582:     /// aka. `interner.parent(def_id)`.\\n583:     pub def_id: I::DefId,\\n584: \\n585:     /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `AliasTerm` without using [`AliasTerm::new_from_args`].\\n586:     #[derive_where(skip(Debug))]\\n587:     _use_alias_term_new_instead: (),\\n588: }\\n589: \\n590: impl<I: Interner> Eq for AliasTerm<I> {}\\n591: \\n592: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\\n593:     pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, def_id: I::DefId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> AliasTerm<I> {\\n594:         interner.debug_assert_args_compatible(def_id, args);\\n595:         AliasTerm { def_id, args, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\\n596:     }\\n597: \\n598:     pub fn new(\\n599:         interner: I,\\n600:         def_id: I::DefId,\\n601:         args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\\n602:     ) -> AliasTerm<I> {\\n603:         let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\\n604:         Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args)\\n605:     }\\n606: \\n607:     pub fn expect_ty(self, interner: I) -> ty::AliasTy<I> {\\n608:         match self.kind(interner) {\\n609:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\\n610:             | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\\n611:             | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\\n612:             | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => {}\\n613:             AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n614:             | AliasTermKind::FreeConst\\n615:             | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\\n616:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => {\\n617:                 panic!(\\\"Cannot turn `UnevaluatedConst` into `AliasTy`\\\")\\n618:             }\\n619:         }\\n620:         ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () }\\n621:     }\\n622: \\n623:     pub fn kind(self, interner: I) -> AliasTermKind {\\n624:         interner.alias_term_kind(self)\\n625:     }\\n626: \\n627:     pub fn to_term(self, interner: I) -> I::Term {\\n628:         match self.kind(interner) {\\n629:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n630:                 interner,\\n631:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Projection,\\n632:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n633:             )\\n634:             .into(),\\n635:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n636:                 interner,\\n637:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Inherent,\\n638:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n639:             )\\n640:             .into(),\\n641:             AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n642:                 interner,\\n643:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Opaque,\\n644:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n645:             )\\n646:             .into(),\\n647:             AliasTermKind::FreeTy => Ty::new_alias(\\n648:                 interner,\\n649:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Free,\\n650:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\\n651:             )\\n652:             .into(),\\n653:             AliasTermKind::FreeConst\\n654:             | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n655:             | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\\n656:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => I::Const::new_unevaluated(\\n657:                 interner,\\n658:                 ty::UnevaluatedConst::new(self.def_id.try_into().unwrap(), self.args),\\n659:             )\\n660:             .into(),\\n661:         }\\n662:     }\\n663: }\\n664: \\n665: /// The following methods work only with (trait) associated term projections.\\n666: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\\n667:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\\n668:         self.args.type_at(0)\\n669:     }\\n670: \\n671:     pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> Self {\\n672:         AliasTerm::new(\\n673:             interner,\\n674:             self.def_id,\\n675:             [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)),\\n676:         )\\n677:     }\\n678: \\n679:     pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\\n680:         assert!(\\n681:             matches!(\\n682:                 self.kind(interner),\\n683:                 AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\\n684:             ),\\n685:             \\\"expected a projection\\\"\\n686:         );\\n687:         interner.parent(self.def_id).try_into().unwrap()\\n688:     }\\n689: \\n690:     /// Extracts the underlying trait reference and own args from this projection.\\n691:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as StreamingIterator>::Item<'a>`,\\n692:     /// then this function would return a `T: StreamingIterator` trait reference and\\n693:     /// `['a]` as the own args.\\n694:     pub fn trait_ref_and_own_args(self, interner: I) -> (TraitRef<I>, I::GenericArgsSlice) {\\n695:         interner.trait_ref_and_own_args_for_alias(self.def_id, self.args)\\n696:     }\\n697: \\n698:     /// Extracts the underlying trait reference from this projection.\\n699:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as Iterator>::Item`,\\n700:     /// then this function would return a `T: Iterator` trait reference.\\n701:     ///\\n702:     /// WARNING: This will drop the args for generic associated types\\n703:     /// consider calling [Self::trait_ref_and_own_args] to get only with inherent associated term projections.\\n718: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\\n719:     /// Transform the generic parameters to have the given `impl` args as the base and the GAT args on top of that.\\n720:     ///\\n721:     /// Does the following transformation:\\n722:     ///\\n723:     /// ```text\\n724:     /// [Self, P_0...P_m] -> [I_0...I_n, P_0...P_m]\\n725:     ///\\n726:     ///     I_i impl args\\n727:     ///     P_j GAT args\\n728:     /// ```\\n729:     pub fn rebase_inherent_args_onto_impl(\\n730:         self,\\n731:         impl_args: I::GenericArgs,\\n732:         interner: I,\\n733:     ) -> I::GenericArgs {\\n734:         debug_assert!(matches!(\\n735:             self.kind(interner),\\n736:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\\n737:         ));\\n738:         interner.mk_args_from_iter(impl_args.iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)))\\n739:     }\\n740: }\\n741: \\n742: impl<I: Interner> From<ty::AliasTy<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\\n743:     fn from(ty: ty::AliasTy<I>) -> Self {\\n744:         AliasTerm { args: ty.args, def_id: ty.def_id, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\\n745:     }\\n746: }\\n747: \\n748: impl<I: Interner> From<ty::UnevaluatedConst<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\\n749:     fn from(ct: ty::UnevaluatedConst<I>) -> Self {\\n750:         AliasTerm { args: ct.args, def_id: ct.def.into(), _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\\n751:     }\\n752: }\\n753: \\n754: /// This kind of predicate has no *direct* correspondent in the\\n755: /// syntax, but it roughly corresponds to the syntactic forms:\\n756: ///\\n757: /// 1. `T: TraitRef<..., Item = Type>`\\n758: /// 2. `<T as TraitRef<...>>::Item == Type` (NYI)\\n759: ///\\n760: /// In particular, form #1 is \\\"desugared\\\" to the combination of a\\n761: /// normal trait predicate (`T: TraitRef<...>`) and one of these\\n762: /// predicates. Form #2 is a broader form in that it also permits\\n763: /// equality between arbitrary types. Processing an instance of\\n764: /// Form #2 eventually yields one of these `ProjectionPredicate`\\n765: /// instances to normalize the LHS.\\n766: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\\n767: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\\n768: #[cfg_attr(\\n769:     feature = \\\"nightly\\\",\\n770:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\\n771: )]\\n772: pub struct ProjectionPredicate<I: Interner> {\\n773:     pub projection_term: AliasTerm<I>,\\n774:     pub term: I::Term,\\n775: }\\n776: \\n777: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ProjectionPredicate<I> {}\\n778: \\n779: impl<I: Interner> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\\n780:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\\n781: }\\n799: \\n800: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ProjectionPredicate<I>> {\\n801:     /// Returns the `DefId` of the trait of the associated item being projected.\\n802:     #[inline]\\n803:     pub self.term)\\n824:     }\\n825: }\\n826: \\n827: /// Used by the new solver to normalize an alias. #[derive_where(Clone, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner, A)]\n19: #[derive_where(Copy; I: Interner, A: Copy)]\n20: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic)]\n21: #[cfg_attr(\n22:     feature = \"nightly\",\n23:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n24: )]\n25: pub struct OutlivesPredicate<I: Interner, A>(pub A, pub I::Region);\n26: \n27: impl<I: Interner, A: Eq> Eq for OutlivesPredicate<I, A> {}\n28: \n29: // FIXME: We manually derive `Lift` because the `derive(Lift_Generic)` doesn't\n30: // understand how to turn `A` to `A::Lifted` in the output `type Lifted`.\n31: impl<I: Interner, U: Interner, A> Lift<U> for OutlivesPredicate<I, A>\n32: where\n33:     A: Lift<U>,\n34:     I::Region: Lift<U, Lifted = U::Region>,\n35: {\n36:     type Lifted = OutlivesPredicate<U, A::Lifted>;\n37: \n38:     fn lift_to_interner(self, cx: U) -> Option<Self::Lifted> {\n39:         Some(OutlivesPredicate(self.0.lift_to_interner(cx)?, self.1.lift_to_interner(cx)?))\n40:     }\n41: }\n42: \n43: /// A complete reference to a trait. These take numerous guises in syntax,\n44: /// but perhaps the most recognizable form is in a where-clause:\n45: /// ```ignore (illustrative)\n46: /// T: Foo<U>\n47: /// ```\n48: /// This would be represented by a trait-reference where the `DefId` is the\n49: /// `DefId` for the trait `Foo` and the args define `T` as parameter 0,\n50: /// and `U` as parameter 1.\n51: ///\n52: /// Trait references also appear in object types like `Foo<U>`, but in\n53: /// that case the `Self` parameter is absent from the generic parameters.\n54: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\n55: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n56: #[cfg_attr(\n57:     feature = \"nightly\",\n58:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n59: )]\n60: pub struct TraitRef<I: Interner> {\n61:     pub def_id: I::TraitId,\n62:     pub args: I::GenericArgs,\n63:     /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `TraitRef` without\n64:     /// calling [`TraitRef::new_from_args`].\n65:     _use_trait_ref_new_instead: (),\n66: }\n67: \n68: impl<I: Interner> Eq for TraitRef<I> {}\n69: \n70: impl<I: Interner> TraitRef<I> {\n71:     pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, trait_def_id: I::TraitId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> Self {\n72:         interner.debug_assert_args_compatible(trait_def_id.into(), args);\n73:         Self { def_id: trait_def_id, args, _use_trait_ref_new_instead: () }\n74:     }\n75: \n76:     pub fn new(\n77:         interner: I,\n78:         trait_def_id: I::TraitId,\n79:         args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\n80:     ) -> Self {\n81:         let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\n82:         Self::new_from_args(interner, trait_def_id, args)\n83:     }\n84: \n85:     pub fn from_assoc(interner: I, trait_id: I::TraitId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> TraitRef<I> {\n86:         let generics = interner.generics_of(trait_id.into());\n87:         TraitRef::new(interner, trait_id, args.iter().take(generics.count()))\n88:     }\n89: \n90:     /// Returns a `TraitRef` of the form `P0: Foo<P1..Pn>` where `Pi`\n91:     /// are the parameters defined on trait.\n92:     pub fn identity(interner: I, def_id: I::TraitId) -> TraitRef<I> {\n93:         TraitRef::new_from_args(\n94:             interner,\n95:             def_id,\n96:             I::GenericArgs::identity_for_item(interner, def_id.into()),\n97:         )\n98:     }\n99: \n100:     pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> Self {\n101:         TraitRef::new(\n102:             interner,\n103:             self.def_id,\n104:             [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)),\n105:         )\n106:     }\n107: \n108:     #[inline]\n109:     pub fn self_ty(&self) -> I::Ty {\n110:         self.args.type_at(0)\n111:     }\n112: }\n113: \n114: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, TraitRef<I>> {\n115:     pub fn self_ty(&self) -> ty::Binder<I, I::Ty> {\n116:         self.map_bound_ref(|tr| tr.self_ty())\n117:     }\n118: \n119:     pub fn def_id(&self) -> I::TraitId {\n120:         self.skip_binder().def_id\n121:     }\n122: \n123:     pub fn to_host_effect_clause(self, cx: I, constness: BoundConstness) -> I::Clause {\n124:         self.map_bound(|trait_ref| {\n125:             ty::ClauseKind::HostEffect(HostEffectPredicate { trait_ref, constness })\n126:         })\n127:         .upcast(cx)\n128:     }\n129: }\n130: \n131: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\n132: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n133: #[cfg_attr(\n134:     feature = \"nightly\",\n135:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n136: )]\n137: pub struct TraitPredicate<I: Interner> {\n138:     pub trait_ref: TraitRef<I>,\n139: \n140:     /// If polarity is Positive: we are proving that the trait is implemented.\n141:     ///\n142:     /// If polarity is Negative: we are proving that a negative impl of this trait\n143:     /// exists. (Note that coherence also checks whether negative impls of supertraits\n144:     /// exist via a series of predicates.)\n145:     pub polarity: PredicatePolarity,\n146: }\n147: \n148: impl<I: {\n159:         self.trait_ref.def_id\n160:     }\n161: \n162:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\n163:         self.trait_ref.self_ty()\n164:     }\n165: }\n166: \n167: impl<I: Interner> self.skip_binder().def_id()\n171:     }\n172: \n173:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> ty::Binder<I, I::Ty> {\n174:         self.map_bound(|trait_ref| trait_ref.self_ty())\n175:     }\n176: \n177: \n189: impl<I: Interner> UpcastFrom<I, ty::Binder<I, TraitRef<I>>> for ty::Binder<I, TraitPredicate<I>> {\n190:     fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<I, TraitRef<I>>, _tcx: I) -> Self {\n191: derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n208: )]\n209: pub enum ImplPolarity {\n210:     /// `impl Trait for Type`\n211:     Positive,\n212:     /// `impl !Trait for Type`\n213:     Negative,\n214:     /// `#[rustc_reservation_impl] impl Trait for Type`\n215:     ///\n216:     /// This is a \"stability hack\", not a real Rust feature.\n217:     /// See #64631 for details.\n218:     Reservation,\n219: }\n220: \n221: impl fmt::Display for ImplPolarity {\n222:     fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\n223:         match self {\n224:             Self::Positive => f.write_str(\"positive\"),\n225:             Self::Negative => f.write_str(\"negative\"),\n226:             Self::Reservation => f.write_str(\"reservation\"),\n227:         }\n228:     }\n229: }\n230: \n231: impl ImplPolarity {\n232:     /// The polarity marker in front of the impl trait ref if applicable.\n233:     pub fn as_str(self) -> &'static str {\n234:         match self {\n235:             Self::Positive => \"\",\n236:             Self::Negative => \"!\",\n237:             Self::Reservation => \"\",\n238:         }\n239:     }\n240: }\n241: \n242: /// Polarity for a trait predicate. May either be negative or positive.\n243: /// Distinguished from [`ImplPolarity`] since we never compute goals with\n244: /// \"reservation\" level.\n245: #[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Debug)]\n246: #[cfg_attr(\n247:     feature = \"nightly\",\n248:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n249: )]\n250: pub enum PredicatePolarity {\n251:     /// `Type: Trait`\n252:     Positive,\n253:     /// `Type: !Trait`\n254:     Negative,\n255: }\n256: \n257: impl PredicatePolarity {\n258:     /// Flips polarity by turning `Positive` into `Negative` and `Negative` into `Positive`.\n259:     pub fn flip(&self) -> PredicatePolarity {\n260:         match self {\n261:             PredicatePolarity::Positive => PredicatePolarity::Negative,\n262:             PredicatePolarity::Negative => PredicatePolarity::Positive,\n263:         }\n264:     }\n265: }\n266: \n267: impl fmt::Display for PredicatePolarity ExistentialPredicate<I: Interner> {\n283:     /// E.g., `Iterator`.\n284:     Trait(ExistentialTraitRef<I>),\n285:     /// E.g., `Iterator::Item = T`.\n286:     Projection(ExistentialProjection<I>),\n287:     /// E.g., `Send`.\n288:     AutoTrait(I::TraitId),\n289: }\n290: \n291: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialPredicate<I> {}\n292: \n293: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialPredicate<I>> {\n294:     /// Given an existential predicate like `?Self: PartialEq<u32>` (e.g., derived from `dyn PartialEq<u32>`),\n295:     /// and a concrete type `self_ty`, returns a full predicate where the existentially quantified variable `?Self`\n296:     /// has been replaced with `self_ty` (e.g., `self_ty: PartialEq<u32>`, in our example).\n297:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> I::Clause {\n298:         match self.skip_binder() {\n299:             ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => self.rebind(tr).with_self_ty(cx, self_ty).upcast(cx),\n300:             ExistentialPredicate::Projection(p) => {\n301:                 self.rebind(p.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty)).upcast(cx)\n302:             }\n303:             ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => {\n304:                 let generics = cx.generics_of(did.into());\n305:                 let trait_ref = if generics.count() == 1 {\n306:                     ty::TraitRef::new(cx, did, [self_ty])\n307:                 } else {\n308:                     // If this is an ill-formed auto trait, then synthesize\n309:                     // new error args for the missing generics.\n310:                     let err_args =\n311:                         GenericArgs::extend_with_error(cx, did.into(), &[self_ty.into()]);\n312:                     ty::TraitRef::new_from_args(cx, did, err_args)\n313:                 };\n314:                 self.rebind(trait_ref).upcast(cx)\n315:             }\n316:         }\n317:     }\n318: }\n319: \n320: /// An existential reference to a trait, where `Self` is erased.\n321: /// For example, the trait object `Trait<'a, 'b, X, Y>` is:\n322: /// ```ignore (illustrative)\n323: /// exists T. T: Trait<'a, 'b, X, Y>\n324: /// ```\n325: /// The generic parameters don't include the erased `Self`, only trait\n326: /// type and lifetime parameters (`[X, Y]` and `['a, 'b]` above).\n327: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\n328: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n329: #[cfg_attr(\n330:     feature = \"nightly\",\n331:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n332: )]\n333: pub struct ExistentialTraitRef<I: Interner> {\n334:     pub def_id: I::TraitId,\n335:     pub args: I::GenericArgs,\n336:     /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `ExistentialTraitRef` without\n337:     /// calling [`ExistentialTraitRef::new_from_args`].\n338:     _use_existential_trait_ref_new_instead: (),\n339: }\n340: \n341: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialTraitRef<I> {}\n342: \n343: impl<I: Interner> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\n344:     pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, trait_def_id: I::TraitId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> Self {\n345:         interner.debug_assert_existential_args_compatible(trait_def_id.into(), args);\n346:         Self { def_id: trait_def_id, args, _use_existential_trait_ref_new_instead: () }\n347:     }\n348: \n349:     pub fn new(\n350:         interner: I,\n351:         trait_def_id: I::TraitId,\n352:         args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\n353:     ) -> Self {\n354:         let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\n355:         Self::new_from_args(interner, trait_def_id, args)\n356:     }\n357: \n358:     pub fn erase_self_ty(interner: I, trait_ref: TraitRef<I>) -> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\n359:         // Assert there is a Self.\n360:         trait_ref.args.type_at(0);\n361: \n362:         ExistentialTraitRef {\n363:             def_id: trait_ref.def_id,\n364:             args: interner.mk_args(&trait_ref.args.as_slice()[1..]),\n365:             _use_existential_trait_ref_new_instead: (),\n366:         }\n367:     }\n368: \n369:     /// Object types don't have a self type specified. Therefore, when\n370:     /// we convert the principal trait-ref into a normal trait-ref,\n371:     /// you must give *some* self type. A common choice is `mk_err()`\n372:     /// or some placeholder type.\n373:     pub fn with_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> TraitRef<I> {\n374:         // otherwise the escaping vars would be captured by the binder\n375:         // debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\n376: \n377:         TraitRef::new(interner, self.def_id, [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter()))\n378:     }\n379: }\n380: \n381: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialTraitRef<I>> {\n382:     pub fn def_id(&self) -> I::TraitId {\n383:         self.skip_binder().def_id\n384:     }\n385: \n386:     /// Object types don't have a self type specified. Therefore, when\n387:     /// we convert the principal trait-ref into a normal trait-ref,\n388:     /// you must give *some* self type. A common choice is `mk_err()`\n389:     /// or some placeholder type.\n390:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, TraitRef<I>> {\n391:         self.map_bound(|trait_ref| trait_ref.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty))\n392:     }\n393: }\n394: \n395: /// A `ProjectionPredicate` for an `ExistentialTraitRef`.\n396: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\n397: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n398: #[cfg_attr(\n399:     feature = \"nightly\",\n400:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n401: )]\n402: pub struct ExistentialProjection<I: Interner> {\n403:     pub def_id: I::DefId,\n404:     pub args: I::GenericArgs,\n405:     pub term: I::Term,\n406: \n407:     /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `ExistentialProjection`\n408:     /// without using [`ExistentialProjection::new_from_args`].\n409:     use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\n410: }\n411: \n412: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ExistentialProjection<I> {}\n413: \n414: impl<I: Interner> ExistentialProjection<I> {\n415:     pub fn new_from_args(\n416:         interner: I,\n417:         def_id: I::DefId,\n418:         args: I::GenericArgs,\n419:         term: I::Term,\n420:     ) -> ExistentialProjection<I> {\n421:         interner.debug_assert_existential_args_compatible(def_id, args);\n422:         Self { def_id, args, term, use_existential_projection_new_instead: () }\n423:     }\n424: \n425:     pub fn new(\n426:         interner: I,\n427:         def_id: I::DefId,\n428:         args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\n429:         term: I::Term,\n430:     ) -> ExistentialProjection<I> {\n431:         let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\n432:         Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args, term)\n433:     }\n434: \n435:     /// Extracts the underlying existential trait reference from this projection.\n436:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `exists T. <T as Iterator>::Item == X`,\n437:     /// then this function would return an `exists T. T: Iterator` existential trait\n438:     /// reference.\n439:     pub fn trait_ref(&self, interner: I) -> ExistentialTraitRef<I> {\n440:         let def_id = interner.parent(self.def_id);\n441:         let args_count = interner.generics_of(def_id).count() - 1;\n442:         let args = interner.mk_args(&self.args.as_slice()[..args_count]);\n443:         ExistentialTraitRef::new_from_args(interner, def_id.try_into().unwrap(), args)\n444:     }\n445: \n446:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\n447:         // otherwise the escaping regions would be captured by the binders\n448:         debug_assert!(!self_ty.has_escaping_bound_vars());\n449: \n450:         ProjectionPredicate {\n451:             projection_term: AliasTerm::new(\n452:                 interner,\n453:                 self.def_id,\n454:                 [self_ty.into()].iter().chain(self.args.iter()),\n455:             ),\n456:             term: self.term,\n457:         }\n458:     }\n459: \n460:     pub fn erase_self_ty(interner: I, projection_predicate: ProjectionPredicate<I>) -> Self {\n461:         // Assert there is a Self.\n462:         projection_predicate.projection_term.args.type_at(0);\n463: \n464:         Self {\n465:             def_id: projection_predicate.projection_term.def_id,\n466:             args: interner.mk_args(&projection_predicate.projection_term.args.as_slice()[1..]),\n467:             term: projection_predicate.term,\n468:             use_existential_projection_new_instead: (),\n469:         }\n470:     }\n471: }\n472: \n473: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ExistentialProjection<I>> {\n474:     pub fn with_self_ty(&self, cx: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> ty::Binder<I, ProjectionPredicate<I>> {\n475:         self.map_bound(|p| p.with_self_ty(cx, self_ty))\n476:     }\n477: \n478:     pub fn item_def_id(&self) -> I::DefId {\n479:         self.skip_binder().def_id\n480:     }\n481: }\n482: \n483: #[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Debug)]\n484: #[cfg_attr(feature = \"nightly\", derive(Encodable, Decodable, HashStable_NoContext))]\n485: pub enum AliasTermKind {\n486:     /// A projection `<Type as Trait>::AssocType`.\n487:     /// Can get normalized away if monomorphic enough.\n488:     ProjectionTy,\n489:     /// An associated type in an inherent `impl`\n490:     InherentTy,\n491:     /// An opaque type (usually from `impl Trait` in type aliases or function return types)\n492:     /// Can only be normalized away in PostAnalysis mode or its defining scope.\n493:     OpaqueTy,\n494:     /// A free type alias that actually checks its trait bounds.\n495:     /// Currently only used if the type alias references opaque types.\n496:     /// Can always be normalized away.\n497:     FreeTy,\n498: \n499:     /// An unevaluated anonymous constants.\n500:     UnevaluatedConst,\n501:     /// An unevaluated const coming from an associated const.\n502:     ProjectionConst,\n503:     /// A top level const item not part of a trait or impl.\n504:     FreeConst,\n505:     /// An associated const in an inherent `impl`\n506:     InherentConst,\n507: }\n508: \n509: impl AliasTermKind {\n510:     pub fn descr(self) -> &'static str {\n511:         match self {\n512:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => \"associated type\",\n513:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => \"associated const\",\n514:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy => \"inherent associated type\",\n515:             AliasTermKind::InherentConst => \"inherent associated const\",\n516:             AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => \"opaque type\",\n517:             AliasTermKind::FreeTy => \"type alias\",\n518:             AliasTermKind::FreeConst => \"unevaluated constant\",\n519:             AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst => \"unevaluated constant\",\n520:         }\n521:     }\n522: \n523:     pub fn is_type(self) -> bool {\n524:         match self {\n525:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\n526:             | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\n527:             | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\n528:             | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => true,\n529: \n530:             AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\n531:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\n532:             | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n533:             | AliasTermKind::FreeConst => false,\n534:         }\n535:     }\n536: }\n537: \n538: impl From<ty::AliasTyKind> for AliasTermKind {\n539:     fn from(value: ty::AliasTyKind) -> Self {\n540:         match value {\n541:             ty::Projection => AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy,\n542:             ty::Opaque => AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy,\n543:             ty::Free => AliasTermKind::FreeTy,\n544:             ty::Inherent => AliasTermKind::InherentTy,\n545:         }\n546:     }\n547: }\n548: \n549: /// Represents the unprojected term of a projection goal.\n550: ///\n551: /// * For a projection, this would be `<Ty as Trait<...>>::N<...>`.\n552: /// * For an inherent projection, this would be `Ty::N<...>`.\n553: /// * For an opaque type, there is no explicit syntax.\n554: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; I: Interner)]\n555: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n556: #[cfg_attr(\n557:     feature = \"nightly\",\n558:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n559: )]\n560: pub struct AliasTerm<I: Interner> {\n561:     /// The parameters of the associated or opaque item.\n562:     ///\n563:     /// For a projection, these are the generic parameters for the trait and the\n564:     /// GAT parameters, if there are any.\n565:     ///\n566:     /// For an inherent projection, they consist of the self type and the GAT parameters,\n567:     /// if there are any.\n568:     ///\n569:     /// For RPIT the generic parameters are for the generics of the function,\n570:     /// while for TAIT it is used for the generic parameters of the alias.\n571:     pub args: I::GenericArgs,\n572: \n573:     /// The `DefId` of the `TraitItem` or `ImplItem` for the associated type `N` depending on whether\n574:     /// this is a projection or an inherent projection or the `DefId` of the `OpaqueType` item if\n575:     /// this is an opaque.\n576:     ///\n577:     /// During codegen, `interner.type_of(def_id)` can be used to get the type of the\n578:     /// underlying type if the type is an opaque.\n579:     ///\n580:     /// Note that if this is an associated type, this is not the `DefId` of the\n581:     /// `TraitRef` containing this associated type, which is in `interner.associated_item(def_id).container`,\n582:     /// aka. `interner.parent(def_id)`.\n583:     pub def_id: I::DefId,\n584: \n585:     /// This field exists to prevent the creation of `AliasTerm` without using [`AliasTerm::new_from_args`].\n586:     #[derive_where(skip(Debug))]\n587:     _use_alias_term_new_instead: (),\n588: }\n589: \n590: impl<I: Interner> Eq for AliasTerm<I> {}\n591: \n592: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\n593:     pub fn new_from_args(interner: I, def_id: I::DefId, args: I::GenericArgs) -> AliasTerm<I> {\n594:         interner.debug_assert_args_compatible(def_id, args);\n595:         AliasTerm { def_id, args, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\n596:     }\n597: \n598:     pub fn new(\n599:         interner: I,\n600:         def_id: I::DefId,\n601:         args: impl IntoIterator<Item: Into<I::GenericArg>>,\n602:     ) -> AliasTerm<I> {\n603:         let args = interner.mk_args_from_iter(args.into_iter().map(Into::into));\n604:         Self::new_from_args(interner, def_id, args)\n605:     }\n606: \n607:     pub fn expect_ty(self, interner: I) -> ty::AliasTy<I> {\n608:         match self.kind(interner) {\n609:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy\n610:             | AliasTermKind::InherentTy\n611:             | AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy\n612:             | AliasTermKind::FreeTy => {}\n613:             AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n614:             | AliasTermKind::FreeConst\n615:             | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\n616:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => {\n617:                 panic!(\"Cannot turn `UnevaluatedConst` into `AliasTy`\")\n618:             }\n619:         }\n620:         ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () }\n621:     }\n622: \n623:     pub fn kind(self, interner: I) -> AliasTermKind {\n624:         interner.alias_term_kind(self)\n625:     }\n626: \n627:     pub fn to_term(self, interner: I) -> I::Term {\n628:         match self.kind(interner) {\n629:             AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy => Ty::new_alias(\n630:                 interner,\n631:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Projection,\n632:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n633:             )\n634:             .into(),\n635:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy => Ty::new_alias(\n636:                 interner,\n637:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Inherent,\n638:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n639:             )\n640:             .into(),\n641:             AliasTermKind::OpaqueTy => Ty::new_alias(\n642:                 interner,\n643:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Opaque,\n644:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n645:             )\n646:             .into(),\n647:             AliasTermKind::FreeTy => Ty::new_alias(\n648:                 interner,\n649:                 ty::AliasTyKind::Free,\n650:                 ty::AliasTy { def_id: self.def_id, args: self.args, _use_alias_ty_new_instead: () },\n651:             )\n652:             .into(),\n653:             AliasTermKind::FreeConst\n654:             | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n655:             | AliasTermKind::UnevaluatedConst\n656:             | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst => I::Const::new_unevaluated(\n657:                 interner,\n658:                 ty::UnevaluatedConst::new(self.def_id.try_into().unwrap(), self.args),\n659:             )\n660:             .into(),\n661:         }\n662:     }\n663: }\n664: \n665: /// The following methods work only with (trait) associated term projections.\n666: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\n667:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\n668:         self.args.type_at(0)\n669:     }\n670: \n671:     pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> Self {\n672:         AliasTerm::new(\n673:             interner,\n674:             self.def_id,\n675:             [self_ty.into()].into_iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)),\n676:         )\n677:     }\n678: \n679:     pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\n680:         assert!(\n681:             matches!(\n682:                 self.kind(interner),\n683:                 AliasTermKind::ProjectionTy | AliasTermKind::ProjectionConst\n684:             ),\n685:             \"expected a projection\"\n686:         );\n687:         interner.parent(self.def_id).try_into().unwrap()\n688:     }\n689: \n690:     /// Extracts the underlying trait reference and own args from this projection.\n691:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as StreamingIterator>::Item<'a>`,\n692:     /// then this function would return a `T: StreamingIterator` trait reference and\n693:     /// `['a]` as the own args.\n694:     pub fn trait_ref_and_own_args(self, interner: I) -> (TraitRef<I>, I::GenericArgsSlice) {\n695:         interner.trait_ref_and_own_args_for_alias(self.def_id, self.args)\n696:     }\n697: \n698:     /// Extracts the underlying trait reference from this projection.\n699:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as Iterator>::Item`,\n700:     /// then this function would return a `T: Iterator` trait reference.\n701:     ///\n702:     /// WARNING: This will drop the args for generic associated types\n703:     /// consider calling [Self::trait_ref_and_own_args] to get those\n704:     /// as well.\n705:     pub fn trait_ref(self, interner: I) -> TraitRef<I> {\n706:         self.trait_ref_and_own_args(interner).0\n707:     }\n708: \n709:     /// Extract the own args from this projection.\n710:     /// For example, if this is a projection of `<T as StreamingIterator>::Item<'a>`,\n711:     /// then this function would return the slice `['a]` as the own args.\n712:     pub fn own_args(self, interner: I) -> I::GenericArgsSlice {\n713:         self.trait_ref_and_own_args(interner).1\n714:     }\n715: }\n716: \n717: /// The following methods work only with inherent associated term projections.\n718: impl<I: Interner> AliasTerm<I> {\n719:     /// Transform the generic parameters to have the given `impl` args as the base and the GAT args on top of that.\n720:     ///\n721:     /// Does the following transformation:\n722:     ///\n723:     /// ```text\n724:     /// [Self, P_0...P_m] -> [I_0...I_n, P_0...P_m]\n725:     ///\n726:     ///     I_i impl args\n727:     ///     P_j GAT args\n728:     /// ```\n729:     pub fn rebase_inherent_args_onto_impl(\n730:         self,\n731:         impl_args: I::GenericArgs,\n732:         interner: I,\n733:     ) -> I::GenericArgs {\n734:         debug_assert!(matches!(\n735:             self.kind(interner),\n736:             AliasTermKind::InherentTy | AliasTermKind::InherentConst\n737:         ));\n738:         interner.mk_args_from_iter(impl_args.iter().chain(self.args.iter().skip(1)))\n739:     }\n740: }\n741: \n742: impl<I: Interner> From<ty::AliasTy<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\n743:     fn from(ty: ty::AliasTy<I>) -> Self {\n744:         AliasTerm { args: ty.args, def_id: ty.def_id, _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\n745:     }\n746: }\n747: \n748: impl<I: Interner> From<ty::UnevaluatedConst<I>> for AliasTerm<I> {\n749:     fn from(ct: ty::UnevaluatedConst<I>) -> Self {\n750:         AliasTerm { args: ct.args, def_id: ct.def.into(), _use_alias_term_new_instead: () }\n751:     }\n752: }\n753: \n754: /// This kind of predicate has no *direct* correspondent in the\n755: /// syntax, but it roughly corresponds to the syntactic forms:\n756: ///\n757: /// 1. `T: TraitRef<..., Item = Type>`\n758: /// 2. `<T as TraitRef<...>>::Item == Type` (NYI)\n759: ///\n760: /// In particular, form #1 is \"desugared\" to the combination of a\n761: /// normal trait predicate (`T: TraitRef<...>`) and one of these\n762: /// predicates. Form #2 is a broader form in that it also permits\n763: /// equality between arbitrary types. Processing an instance of\n764: /// Form #2 eventually yields one of these `ProjectionPredicate`\n765: /// instances to normalize the LHS.\n766: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\n767: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n768: #[cfg_attr(\n769:     feature = \"nightly\",\n770:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n771: )]\n772: pub struct ProjectionPredicate<I: Interner> {\n773:     pub projection_term: AliasTerm<I>,\n774:     pub term: I::Term,\n775: }\n776: \n777: impl<I: Interner> Eq for ProjectionPredicate<I> {}\n778: \n779: impl<I: Interner> ProjectionPredicate<I> {\n780:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\n781:         self.projection_term.self_ty()\n782:     }\n783: \n784:  self.projection_term.trait_def_id(interner)\n793:     }\n794: \n795:     pub fn def_id(self) -> I::DefId {\n796:         self.projection_term.def_id\n797:     }\n798: }\n799: \n800: impl<I: Interner> ty::Binder<I, ProjectionPredicate<I>> {\n801:     /// Returns the `DefId` of the trait of the associated item being projected.\n802:     #[inline]\n803:     pub fn trait_def_id(&self, cx: I) -> I::TraitId {\n804:         self.skip_binder().projection_term.trait_def_id(cx)\n805:     }\n806: \n807:     pub fn term(&self) -> ty::Binder<I, I::Term> {\n808:         self.map_bound(|predicate| predicate.term)\n809:     }\n810: \n811:     /// The `DefId` of the `TraitItem` for the associated type.\n812:     ///\n813:     /// Note that this is not the `DefId` of the `TraitRef` containing this\n814:     /// associated type, which is in `tcx.associated_item(projection_def_id()).container`.\n815:     pub fn item_def_id(&self) -> I::DefId {\n816:         // Ok to skip binder since trait `DefId` does not care about regions.\n817:         self.skip_binder().projection_term.def_id\n818:     }\n819: }\n820: \n821: impl<I: Interner> fmt::Debug for ProjectionPredicate<I> {\n822:     fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\n823:         write!(f, \"ProjectionPredicate({:?}, {:?})\", self.projection_term, self.term)\n824:     }\n825: }\n826: \n827: /// Used by the new solver to normalize an alias. This always expects the `term` to\n828: /// be an unconstrained inference variable which is used as the output.\n829: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq; I: Interner)]\n830: #[derive(TypeVisitable_Generic, TypeFoldable_Generic, Lift_Generic)]\n831: #[cfg_attr(\n832:     feature = \"nightly\",\n833:     derive(Decodable_NoContext, Encodable_NoContext, HashStable_NoContext)\n834: )]\n835: pub struct NormalizesTo<I: Interner> {\n836:     pub alias: AliasTerm<I>,\n837:     pub term: I::Term,\n838: }\n839: \n840: impl<I: Interner> Eq for NormalizesTo<I> {}\n841: \n842: impl<I: Interner> NormalizesTo<I> {\n843:     pub fn self_ty(self) -> I::Ty {\n844:         self.alias.self_ty()\n845:     }\n846: \n847:     pub fn with_replaced_self_ty(self, interner: I, self_ty: I::Ty) -> NormalizesTo<I> {\n848:         Self { alias: self.alias.with_replaced_self_ty(interner, self_ty), ..self }\n849:     }\n850: \n851:     pub fn trait_def_id(self, interner: I) -> I::TraitId {\n852:         self.alias.trait_def_id(interner)\n853:     }\n854: \n855:     pub fn def_id(self) -> I::DefId {\n856:         self.alias.def_id\n857:     }\n858: }\n859: \n860: impl<I: Interner> fmt::Debug for NormalizesTo<I> {\n861:     fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {\n862:         write!(f, \"NormalizesTo({:?}, {:?})\", self.alias, self.term)\n863:     }\n864: }\n865: \n866: #[derive_where(Clone, Copy, Hash, PartialEq, Debug; Hash)]\\n155: pub struct TyConstId(usize, ThreadLocalIndex);\\n156: \\n157: /// Represents a constant in MIR\\n158: #[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Serialize)]\\n159: pub struct MirConst \\n218: type Ident = Opaque;\\n219: \\n220: #[derive(Clone, Debug, Eq, PartialEq, Hash, Serialize)]\\n221: pub struct Region {\\n222:     pub kind: RegionKind,\\n223: }\\n224: \\n225: #[derive(Clone, \\n496:     /// Returns the type and mutability of `*ty` for builtin types.\\n497:     ///\\n498:     /// The parameter `explicit` indicates if this is an *explicit* dereference.\\n499:     /// Some types -- notably raw ptrs -- can only be dereferenced explicitly.\\n500:     pub fn builtin_deref(&self, explicit: bool) -> Option<TypeAndMut> {\\n501:         match self.rigid()? {\\n502: }\\n1328:         let raw = self.raw_bytes()?;\\n1329:         read_target_int(&raw)\\n1330:     }\\n1331: \\n1332:     /// Read this allocation and try to convert it to a boolean.\\n1333:     pub fn read_bool(&self) -> Result<bool, Error> {\\n1334:         match self.read_int()? {\\n1335:             0 => Ok(false),\\n1336:             1 => Ok(true),\\n1337:             val => #[inline]\n389:     pub fn is_float(&self) -> bool {\n390:         matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Float(_)))\n391:     }\n392: \n393:     #[inline]\n394:     pub fn is_integral(&self) -> bool {\n395:         matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Int(_) | RigidTy::Uint(_)))\n396:     }\n397: \n398:     #[inline]\n399:     pub fn is_numeric(&self) -> bool {\n400:         self.is_integral() || self.is_float()\n401:     }\n402: \n403:     #[inline]\n404:     pub fn is_signed(&self) -> bool {\n405:         matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Int(_)))\n406:     }\n407: \n408:     #[inline]\n409:     pub fn is_str(&self) -> bool {\n410:         *self == TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Str)\n411:     }\n412: \n413:     #[inline]\n414:     pub fn is_cstr(&self) -> bool {\n415:         let TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Adt(def, _)) = self else {\n416:             return false;\n417:         };\n418:         with(|cx| cx.adt_is_cstr(*def))\n419:     }\n420: \n421:     #[inline]\n422: #[inline]\n452:     pub fn is_coroutine(&self) -> bool {\n453:         matches!(self, TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Coroutine(..)))\n454:     }\n455: \n456:     #[inline]\n457:     pub fn is_closure(&self)   TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Adt(def, _)) => def.is_box(),\n465:             _ => false,\n466:         }\n467:     }\n468: \n469:     #[inline]\n470:     pub fn is_simd(&self) -> bool {\n471: predicates.first()\n478:             {\n479:                 Some(Binder { value: trait_ref.clone(), bound_vars: bound_vars.clone() })\n480:             } else {\n481:                 None\n482:             }\n483:         } else {\n484:             None\n485:         }\n486:     }\n487: \n488:     /// Returns the type of `ty[i]` for builtin types.\n489:     pub fn builtin_index(&self) -> Option<Ty> {\n490:         match self.rigid()? {\n491:             RigidTy::Array(ty, _) | RigidTy::Slice(ty) => Some(*ty),\n492:             _ => None,\n493:         }\n494:     }\n495: \n496:     /// Returns the type and mutability of `*ty` for builtin types.\n497:     ///\n498:     /// The parameter `explicit` indicates if this is an *explicit* dereference.\n499:     /// Some types -- notably raw ptrs -- can only be dereferenced explicitly.\n500:     pub fn builtin_deref(&self, explicit: bool) -> Option<TypeAndMut> {\n501:         match self.rigid()? {\n502:             RigidTy::Adt(def, args) if def.is_box() => {\n503:                 Some(TypeAndMut { ty: *args.0.first()?.ty()?, mutability: Mutability::Not })\n504:             }\n505:             RigidTy::Ref(_, ty, mutability) => {\n506:                 Some(TypeAndMut { ty: *ty, mutability: *mutability })\n507:             }\n508:             RigidTy::RawPtr(ty, mutability) if explicit => {\n509:                 Some(TypeAndMut { ty: *ty, mutability: *mutability })\n510:             }\n511:             _ => None,\n512:         }\n513:     }\n514: \n515:     /// Get the function signature for function like types (Fn, FnPtr, and Closure)\n516:     pub fn fn_sig(&self) -> Option<PolyFnSig> {\n517:         match self {\n518:             TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::FnDef(def, args)) => Some(with(|cx| cx.fn_sig(*def, args))),\n519:             TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::FnPtr(sig)) => Some(sig.clone()),\n520:             TyKind::RigidTy(RigidTy::Closure(_def, args)) => Some(with(|cx| cx.closure_sig(args))),\n521:             _ => None,\n522:         }\n523:     }\n524: \n525:     /// Get the discriminant type for this type.\n526:     pub fn discriminant_ty(&self) -> Option<Ty> {\n527:         self.rigid().map(|ty| with(|cx| cx.rigid_ty_discriminant_ty(ty)))\n528:     }\n529: \n530:     /// Deconstruct a function type if this is one.\n531:     pub fn fn_def(&self) ->     ///\\n97:     /// Given `T: Trait` predicate it returns `T: !Trait` and given `T: !Trait` returns `T: Trait`.\\n98:     pub fn flip_polarity(self, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Option<Predicate<'tcx>> \\n242: impl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::Clauses<TyCtxt<'tcx>> for ty::Clauses<'tcx> {}\\n243: \\n244: #[extension(pub trait ExistentialPredicateStableCmpExt<'tcx>)]\\n245: impl<'tcx> ExistentialPredicate<'tcx> {\\n246:     /// Compares via an ordering that will not change if modules are reordered or other changes are\\n247:     /// made to the tree. In particular, this ordering is preserved across incremental compilations.\\n248:     fn stable_cmp(&self, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>, other: &Self) -> Ordering {\\n249:         match (*self, *other) {\\n250:             (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => Ordering::Equal,\\n251:             (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref b)) => {\\n252:                 tcx.def_path_hash(a.def_id).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(b.def_id))\\n253:             }\\n254:             (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref b)) => {\\n255:                 tcx.def_path_hash(*a).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(*b))\\n256:             }\\n257:             (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\\n258:             (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => {\\n259:                 Ordering::Greater\\n260:             }\\n261:             (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\\n262:             (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_), _) => Ordering::Greater,\\n263:         }\\n264:     }\\n265: }\\n266: \\n267: pub type PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>;\\n268: \\n269: impl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::BoundExistentialPredicates<TyCtxt<'tcx>>\\n270:     for &'tcx ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>\\n271: {\\n272:     fn principal_def_id(self) -> Option<DefId> {\\n273:         self.principal_def_id()\\n274:     }\\n275: \\n276:     fn principal(self) -> Option<ty::PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>> {\\n277:         self.principal()\\n278:     }\\n279: \\n280:     fn auto_traits(self) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = DefId> {\\n281:         self.auto_traits()\\n282:     }\\n283: \\n284:     fn projection_bounds(\\n285:         self,\\n286:     ) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\\n287:         self.projection_bounds()\\n288:     }\\n289: }\\n290: \\n291: impl<'tcx> ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\\n292:     /// Returns the \\\"principal `DefId`\\\" of this set of existential predicates.\\n293:     ///\\n294:     /// A Rust trait object type consists (in addition to a lifetime bound)\\n295:     /// of a set of trait bounds, which are separated into any number\\n296:     /// of auto-trait bounds, and at most one non-auto-trait bound. The\\n297:     /// non-auto-trait bound is called the \\\"principal\\\" of the trait\\n298:     /// object.\\n299:     ///\\n300:     /// Only the principal can have methods or type parameters (because\\n301:     /// auto traits can have neither of them). This is important, because\\n302:     /// it means the auto traits can be treated as an unordered set (methods\\n303:     /// would force an order for the vtable, while relating traits with\\n304:     /// type parameters without knowing the order to relate them in is\\n305:     /// a rather non-trivial task).\\n306:     ///\\n307:     /// For example, in the trait object `dyn std::fmt::Debug + Sync`, the\\n308:     /// principal bound is `Some(std::fmt::Debug)`, while the auto-trait bounds\\n309:     /// are the set `{Sync}`.\\n310:     ///\\n311:     /// It is also possible to have a \\\"trivial\\\" trait object that\\n312:     /// consists only of auto traits, with no principal - for example,\\n313:     /// `dyn Send + Sync`. In that case, the set of auto-trait bounds\\n314:     /// is `{Send, Sync}`, while there is no principal. These trait objects\\n315:     /// have a \\\"trivial\\\" vtable consisting of just the size, alignment,\\n316:     /// and destructor.\\n317:     pub fn principal(&self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>> {\\n318:         self[0]\\n319:             .map_bound(|this| match this {\\n320:                 ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => Some(tr),\\n321:                 _ => None,\\n322:             })\\n323:             .transpose()\\n324:     }\\n325: \\n326:     pub fn principal_def_id(&self) -> Option<DefId> {\\n327:         self.principal().map(|trait_ref| trait_ref.skip_binder().def_id)\\n328:     }\\n329: \\n330:     #[inline]\\n331:     pub fn projection_bounds(\\n332:         &self,\\n333:     ) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\\n334:         self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| {\\n335:             predicate\\n336:                 .map_bound(|pred| match pred {\\n337:                     ExistentialPredicate::Projection(projection) => Some(projection),\\n338:                     _ => None,\\n339:                 })\\n340:                 .transpose()\\n341:         })\\n342:     }\\n343: \\n344:     #[inline]\\n345:     pub fn auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = DefId> {\\n346:         self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| match predicate.skip_binder() {\\n347:             ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => Some(did),\\n348:             _ => None,\\n349:         })\\n350:     }\\n351: \\n352:     pub fn without_auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\\n353:         self.iter().filter(|predicate| {\\n354:             !matches!(predicate.as_ref().skip_binder(), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_))\\n355:         })\\n356:     }\\n357: }\\n358: \\n359: pub type PolyTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitRef<'tcx>>;\\n360: pub type PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>;\\n361: pub type PolyExistentialProjection<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>;\\n362: \\n363: impl<'tcx> Clause<'tcx> {\\n364:     /// Performs a instantiation suitable for going from a\\n365:     /// poly-trait-ref to supertraits that must hold if that\\n366:     /// poly-trait-ref holds. This is slightly different from a normal\\n367:     /// instantiation in terms of what happens with bound regions. See\\n368:     /// lengthy comment below for details.\\n369:     pub fn instantiate_supertrait(\\n370:         self,\\n371:         tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>,\\n372:         trait_ref: ty::PolyTraitRef<'tcx>,\\n373:     ) -> Clause<'tcx> {\\n374:         // The interaction between HRTB and supertraits is not entirely\\n375:         // obvious. Let me walk you (and myself) through an example.\\n376:         //\\n377:         // Let's start with an easy case. Consider two traits:\\n378:         //\\n379:         //     trait Foo<'a>: Bar<'a,'a> { }\\n380:         //     trait Bar<'b,'c> { }\\n381:         //\\n382:         // Now, if we have a trait reference `for<'x> T: Foo<'x>`, then\\n383:         // we can deduce that `for<'x> T: Bar<'x,'x>`. Basically, if we\\n384:         // knew that `Foo<'x>` (for any 'x) then we also know that\\n385:         // `Bar<'x,'x>` (for any 'x). This more-or-less falls out from\\n386:         // normal instantiation.\\n387:         //\\n388:         // In terms of why this is sound, the idea is that whenever there\\n389:         // is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>`, it must show that `T:Bar<'a,'a>`\\n390:         // holds. So if there is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>` that applies to\\n391:         // all `'a`, then we must know that `T:Bar<'a,'a>` holds for all\\n392:         // `'a`.\\n393:         //\\n394:         // Another example to be careful of is this:\\n395:         //\\n396:         //     trait Foo1<'a>: for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b> { }\\n397:         //     trait Bar1<'b,'c> { }\\n398:         //\\n399:         // Here, if we have `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`, then what do we know?\\n400:         // The answer is that we know `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`. The\\n401:         // reason is similar to the previous example: any impl of\\n402:         // `T:Foo1<'x>` must show that `for<'b> T: Bar1<'x, 'b>`. So\\n403:         // basically we would want to collapse the bound lifetimes from\\n404:         // the input (`trait_ref`) and the supertraits.\\n405:         //\\n406:         // To achieve this in practice is fairly straightforward. Let's\\n407:         // consider the more complicated scenario:\\n408:         //\\n409:         // - We start out with `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`. In this case, `'x`\\n410:         //   has a De Bruijn index of 1. We want to produce `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`,\\n411:         //   where both `'x` and `'b` would have a DB index of 1.\\n412:         //   The instantiation from the input trait-ref is therefore going to be\\n413:         //   `'a => 'x` (where `'x` has a DB index of 1).\\n414:         // - The supertrait-ref is `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, where `'a` is an\\n415:         //   early-bound parameter and `'b` is a late-bound parameter with a\\n416:         //   DB index of 1.\\n417:         // - If we replace `'a` with `'x` from the input, it too will have\\n418:         //   a DB index of 1, and thus we'll have `for<'x,'b> Bar1<'x,'b>`\\n419:         //   just as we wanted.\\n420:         //\\n421:         // There is only one catch. If we just apply the instantiation `'a\\n422:         // => 'x` to `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, the instantiation code will\\n423:         // adjust the DB index because we instantiating into a binder (it\\n424:         // tries to be so smart...) resulting in `for<'x> for<'b>\\n425:         // Bar1<'x,'b>` (we have no syntax for this, so use your\\n426:         // imagination). Basically the 'x will have DB index of 2 and 'b\\n427:         // will have DB index of 1. Not quite what we want. So we apply\\n428:         // the instantiation to the *contents* of the trait reference,\\n429:         // rather than the trait reference itself (put another way, the\\n430:         // instantiation code expects equal binding levels in the values\\n431:         // from the instantiation and the value being instantiated into, and\\n432:         // this trick achieves that).\\n433: \\n434:         // Working through the second example:\\n435:         // trait_ref: for<'x> T: Foo1<'^0.0>; args: [T, '^0.0]\\n436:         // predicate: for<'b> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0>; args: [Self, 'a, '^0.0]\\n437:         // We want to end up with:\\n438:         //     for<'x, 'b> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\\n439:         // To do this:\\n440:         // 1) We must shift all bound vars in predicate by the length\\n441:         //    of trait ref's bound vars. So, we would end up with predicate like\\n442:         //    Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\\n443:         // 2) We can then apply the trait args to this, ending up with\\n444:         //    T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\\n445:         // 3) Finally, to create the final bound vars, we concatenate the bound\\n446:         //    vars of the trait ref with those of the predicate:\\n447:         //    ['x, 'b]\\n448:         let bound_pred = self.kind();\\n449:         let pred_bound_vars = bound_pred.bound_vars();\\n450:         let trait_bound_vars = trait_ref.bound_vars();\\n451:         // 1) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0> -> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\\n452:         let shifted_pred =\\n453:             tcx.shift_bound_var_indices(trait_bound_vars.len(), bound_pred.skip_binder());\\n454:         // 2) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1> -> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\\n455:         let new = EarlyBinder::bind(shifted_pred).instantiate(tcx, trait_ref.skip_binder().args);\\n456:         // 3) ['x] + ['b] -> ['x, 'b]\\n457:         let bound_vars =\\n458:             tcx.mk_bound_variable_kinds_from_iter(trait_bound_vars.iter().chain(pred_bound_vars));\\n459: \\n460:         // FIXME: Is it really perf sensitive to use reuse_or_mk_predicate here?\\n461:         tcx.reuse_or_mk_predicate(\\n462:             self.as_predicate(),\\n463:             ty::Binder::bind_with_vars(PredicateKind::Clause(new), bound_vars),\\n464:         )\\n465:         .expect_clause()\\n466:     }\\n467: }\\n468: \\n469: impl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, PredicateKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\\n470:     fn upcast_from(from: PredicateKind<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\\n471:         ty::Binder::dummy(from).upcast(tcx)\\n472:     }\\n473: }\\n474: \\n475: impl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\\n476:     fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\\n477:         tcx.mk_predicate(from)\\n478:     }\\n479: }\\n480: \\n481: impl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ClauseKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\\n482:     fn upcast_from(from: ClauseKind<'tcx>, tcx: \\n242: impl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::Clauses<TyCtxt<'tcx>> for ty::Clauses<'tcx> {}\\n243: \\n244: #[extension(pub trait ExistentialPredicateStableCmpExt<'tcx>)]\\n245: impl<'tcx> ExistentialPredicate<'tcx> {\\n246:     /// Compares via an ordering that will not change if modules are reordered or other changes are\\n247:     /// made to the tree. In particular, this ordering is preserved across incremental compilations.\\n248:     fn stable_cmp(&self, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>, other: &Self) -> Ordering {\\n249:         match (*self, *other) {\\n250:             (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => Ordering::Equal,\\n251:             (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::Projection(ref b)) => {\\n252:                 tcx.def_path_hash(a.def_id).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(b.def_id))\\n253:             }\\n254:             (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref a), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(ref b)) => {\\n255:                 tcx.def_path_hash(*a).cmp(&tcx.def_path_hash(*b))\\n256:             }\\n257:             (ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\\n258:             (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), ExistentialPredicate::Trait(_)) => {\\n259:                 Ordering::Greater\\n260:             }\\n261:             (ExistentialPredicate::Projection(_), _) => Ordering::Less,\\n262:             (ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_), _) => Ordering::Greater,\\n263:         }\\n264:     }\\n265: }\\n266: \\n267: pub type PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>;\\n268: \\n269: impl<'tcx> rustc_type_ir::inherent::BoundExistentialPredicates<TyCtxt<'tcx>>\\n270:     for &'tcx ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>>\\n271: {\\n272:     fn principal_def_id(self) -> Option<DefId> {\\n273:         self.principal_def_id()\\n274:     }\\n275: \\n276:     fn principal(self) -> Option<ty::PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>> {\\n277:         self.principal()\\n278:     }\\n279: \\n280:     fn auto_traits(self) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = DefId> {\\n281:         self.auto_traits()\\n282:     }\\n283: \\n284:     fn projection_bounds(\\n285:         self,\\n286:     ) -> impl IntoIterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\\n287:         self.projection_bounds()\\n288:     }\\n289: }\\n290: \\n291: impl<'tcx> ty::List<ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\\n292:     /// Returns the \\\"principal `DefId`\\\" of this set of existential predicates.\\n293:     ///\\n294:     /// A Rust trait object type consists (in addition to a lifetime bound)\\n295:     /// of a set of trait bounds, which are separated into any number\\n296:     /// of auto-trait bounds, and at most one non-auto-trait bound. The\\n297:     /// non-auto-trait bound is called the \\\"principal\\\" of the trait\\n298:     /// object.\\n299:     ///\\n300:     /// Only the principal can have methods or type parameters (because\\n301:     /// auto traits can have neither of them). This is important, because\\n302:     /// it means the auto traits can be treated as an unordered set (methods\\n303:     /// would force an order for the vtable, while relating traits with\\n304:     /// type parameters without knowing the order to relate them in is\\n305:     /// a rather non-trivial task).\\n306:     ///\\n307:     /// For example, in the trait object `dyn std::fmt::Debug + Sync`, the\\n308:     /// principal bound is `Some(std::fmt::Debug)`, while the auto-trait bounds\\n309:     /// are the set `{Sync}`.\\n310:     ///\\n311:     /// It is also possible to have a \\\"trivial\\\" trait object that\\n312:     /// consists only of auto traits, with no principal - for example,\\n313:     /// `dyn Send + Sync`. In that case, the set of auto-trait bounds\\n314:     /// is `{Send, Sync}`, while there is no principal. These trait objects\\n315:     /// have a \\\"trivial\\\" vtable consisting of just the size, alignment,\\n316:     /// and destructor.\\n317:     pub fn principal(&self) -> Option<ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>> {\\n318:         self[0]\\n319:             .map_bound(|this| match this {\\n320:                 ExistentialPredicate::Trait(tr) => Some(tr),\\n321:                 _ => None,\\n322:             })\\n323:             .transpose()\\n324:     }\\n325: \\n326:     pub fn principal_def_id(&self) -> Option<DefId> {\\n327:         self.principal().map(|trait_ref| trait_ref.skip_binder().def_id)\\n328:     }\\n329: \\n330:     #[inline]\\n331:     pub fn projection_bounds(\\n332:         &self,\\n333:     ) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>> {\\n334:         self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| {\\n335:             predicate\\n336:                 .map_bound(|pred| match pred {\\n337:                     ExistentialPredicate::Projection(projection) => Some(projection),\\n338:                     _ => None,\\n339:                 })\\n340:                 .transpose()\\n341:         })\\n342:     }\\n343: \\n344:     #[inline]\\n345:     pub fn auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = DefId> {\\n346:         self.iter().filter_map(|predicate| match predicate.skip_binder() {\\n347:             ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(did) => Some(did),\\n348:             _ => None,\\n349:         })\\n350:     }\\n351: \\n352:     pub fn without_auto_traits(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = ty::PolyExistentialPredicate<'tcx>> {\\n353:         self.iter().filter(|predicate| {\\n354:             !matches!(predicate.as_ref().skip_binder(), ExistentialPredicate::AutoTrait(_))\\n355:         })\\n356:     }\\n357: }\\n358: \\n359: pub type PolyTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, TraitRef<'tcx>>;\\n360: pub type PolyExistentialTraitRef<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialTraitRef<'tcx>>;\\n361: pub type PolyExistentialProjection<'tcx> = ty::Binder<'tcx, ExistentialProjection<'tcx>>;\\n362: \\n363: impl<'tcx> Clause<'tcx> {\\n364:     /// Performs a instantiation suitable for going from a\\n365:     /// poly-trait-ref to supertraits that must hold if that\\n366:     /// poly-trait-ref holds. This is slightly different from a normal\\n367:     /// instantiation in terms of what happens with bound regions. See\\n368:     /// lengthy comment below for details.\\n369:     pub fn instantiate_supertrait(\\n370:         self,\\n371:         tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>,\\n372:         trait_ref: ty::PolyTraitRef<'tcx>,\\n373:     ) -> Clause<'tcx> {\\n374:         // The interaction between HRTB and supertraits is not entirely\\n375:         // obvious. Let me walk you (and myself) through an example.\\n376:         //\\n377:         // Let's start with an easy case. Consider two traits:\\n378:         //\\n379:         //     trait Foo<'a>: Bar<'a,'a> { }\\n380:         //     trait Bar<'b,'c> { }\\n381:         //\\n382:         // Now, if we have a trait reference `for<'x> T: Foo<'x>`, then\\n383:         // we can deduce that `for<'x> T: Bar<'x,'x>`. Basically, if we\\n384:         // knew that `Foo<'x>` (for any 'x) then we also know that\\n385:         // `Bar<'x,'x>` (for any 'x). This more-or-less falls out from\\n386:         // normal instantiation.\\n387:         //\\n388:         // In terms of why this is sound, the idea is that whenever there\\n389:         // is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>`, it must show that `T:Bar<'a,'a>`\\n390:         // holds. So if there is an impl of `T:Foo<'a>` that applies to\\n391:         // all `'a`, then we must know that `T:Bar<'a,'a>` holds for all\\n392:         // `'a`.\\n393:         //\\n394:         // Another example to be careful of is this:\\n395:         //\\n396:         //     trait Foo1<'a>: for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b> { }\\n397:         //     trait Bar1<'b,'c> { }\\n398:         //\\n399:         // Here, if we have `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`, then what do we know?\\n400:         // The answer is that we know `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`. The\\n401:         // reason is similar to the previous example: any impl of\\n402:         // `T:Foo1<'x>` must show that `for<'b> T: Bar1<'x, 'b>`. So\\n403:         // basically we would want to collapse the bound lifetimes from\\n404:         // the input (`trait_ref`) and the supertraits.\\n405:         //\\n406:         // To achieve this in practice is fairly straightforward. Let's\\n407:         // consider the more complicated scenario:\\n408:         //\\n409:         // - We start out with `for<'x> T: Foo1<'x>`. In this case, `'x`\\n410:         //   has a De Bruijn index of 1. We want to produce `for<'x,'b> T: Bar1<'x,'b>`,\\n411:         //   where both `'x` and `'b` would have a DB index of 1.\\n412:         //   The instantiation from the input trait-ref is therefore going to be\\n413:         //   `'a => 'x` (where `'x` has a DB index of 1).\\n414:         // - The supertrait-ref is `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, where `'a` is an\\n415:         //   early-bound parameter and `'b` is a late-bound parameter with a\\n416:         //   DB index of 1.\\n417:         // - If we replace `'a` with `'x` from the input, it too will have\\n418:         //   a DB index of 1, and thus we'll have `for<'x,'b> Bar1<'x,'b>`\\n419:         //   just as we wanted.\\n420:         //\\n421:         // There is only one catch. If we just apply the instantiation `'a\\n422:         // => 'x` to `for<'b> Bar1<'a,'b>`, the instantiation code will\\n423:         // adjust the DB index because we instantiating into a binder (it\\n424:         // tries to be so smart...) resulting in `for<'x> for<'b>\\n425:         // Bar1<'x,'b>` (we have no syntax for this, so use your\\n426:         // imagination). Basically the 'x will have DB index of 2 and 'b\\n427:         // will have DB index of 1. Not quite what we want. So we apply\\n428:         // the instantiation to the *contents* of the trait reference,\\n429:         // rather than the trait reference itself (put another way, the\\n430:         // instantiation code expects equal binding levels in the values\\n431:         // from the instantiation and the value being instantiated into, and\\n432:         // this trick achieves that).\\n433: \\n434:         // Working through the second example:\\n435:         // trait_ref: for<'x> T: Foo1<'^0.0>; args: [T, '^0.0]\\n436:         // predicate: for<'b> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0>; args: [Self, 'a, '^0.0]\\n437:         // We want to end up with:\\n438:         //     for<'x, 'b> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\\n439:         // To do this:\\n440:         // 1) We must shift all bound vars in predicate by the length\\n441:         //    of trait ref's bound vars. So, we would end up with predicate like\\n442:         //    Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\\n443:         // 2) We can then apply the trait args to this, ending up with\\n444:         //    T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\\n445:         // 3) Finally, to create the final bound vars, we concatenate the bound\\n446:         //    vars of the trait ref with those of the predicate:\\n447:         //    ['x, 'b]\\n448:         let bound_pred = self.kind();\\n449:         let pred_bound_vars = bound_pred.bound_vars();\\n450:         let trait_bound_vars = trait_ref.bound_vars();\\n451:         // 1) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.0> -> Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1>\\n452:         let shifted_pred =\\n453:             tcx.shift_bound_var_indices(trait_bound_vars.len(), bound_pred.skip_binder());\\n454:         // 2) Self: Bar1<'a, '^0.1> -> T: Bar1<'^0.0, '^0.1>\\n455:         let new = EarlyBinder::bind(shifted_pred).instantiate(tcx, trait_ref.skip_binder().args);\\n456:         // 3) ['x] + ['b] -> ['x, 'b]\\n457:         let bound_vars =\\n458:             tcx.mk_bound_variable_kinds_from_iter(trait_bound_vars.iter().chain(pred_bound_vars));\\n459: \\n460:         // FIXME: Is it really perf sensitive to use reuse_or_mk_predicate here?\\n461:         tcx.reuse_or_mk_predicate(\\n462:             self.as_predicate(),\\n463:             ty::Binder::bind_with_vars(PredicateKind::Clause(new), bound_vars),\\n464:         )\\n465:         .expect_clause()\\n466:     }\\n467: }\\n468: \\n469: impl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, PredicateKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\\n470:     fn upcast_from(from: PredicateKind<'tcx>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\\n471:         ty::Binder::dummy(from).upcast(tcx)\\n472:     }\\n473: }\\n474: \\n475: impl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\\n476:     fn upcast_from(from: ty::Binder<'tcx, PredicateKind<'tcx>>, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Self {\\n477:         tcx.mk_predicate(from)\\n478:     }\\n479: }\\n480: \\n481: impl<'tcx> UpcastFrom<TyCtxt<'tcx>, ClauseKind<'tcx>> for Predicate<'tcx> {\\n482:     fn upcast_from(from: ClauseKind<'tcx>, tcx:
